@article{seifrid2022autonomouschemicalexperiments,
    author = "Seifrid, Martin and Pollice, Robert and Aguilar-Granda, Andrés and Chan, Zamyla Morgan and Hotta, Kazuhiro and Ser, Cher Tian and Vestfrid, Jenya and Wu, Tony C. and Aspuru-Guzik, Alán",
    title = "Autonomous Chemical Experiments: Challenges and Perspectives on Establishing a Self-Driving Lab",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "Accounts of Chemical Research",
    volume = "55",
    pages = "2454-2466",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220",
    publisher = "American Chemical Society (ACS)",
    issue = "17",
    issn = "0001-4842"
}


Abstract:
Conspectus We must accelerate the pace at which we make technological advancements to address climate change and disease risks worldwide. This swifter pace of discovery requires faster research and development cycles enabled by better integration between hypothesis generation, design, experimentation, and data analysis. Typical research cycles take months to years. However, data-driven automated laboratories, or self-driving laboratories, can significantly accelerate molecular and materials discovery. Recently, substantial advancements have been made in the areas of machine learning and optimization algorithms that have allowed researchers to extract valuable knowledge from multidimensional data sets. Machine learning models can be trained on large data sets from the literature or databases, but their performance can often be hampered by a lack of negative results or metadata. In contrast, data generated by self-driving laboratories can be information-rich, containing precise details of the experimental conditions and metadata. Consequently, much larger amounts of high-quality data are gathered in self-driving laboratories. When placed in open repositories, this data can be used by the research community to reproduce experiments, for more in-depth analysis, or as the basis for further investigation. Accordingly, high-quality open data sets will increase the accessibility and reproducibility of science, which is sorely needed. In this Account, we describe our efforts to build a self-driving lab for the development of a new class of materials: organic semiconductor lasers (OSLs). Since they have only recently been demonstrated, little is known about the molecular and material design rules for thin-film, electrically-pumped OSL devices as compared to other technologies such as organic light-emitting diodes or organic photovoltaics. To realize high-performing OSL materials, we are developing a flexible system for automated synthesis via iterative Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling reactions. This automated synthesis platform is directly coupled to the analysis and purification capabilities. Subsequently, the molecules of interest can be transferred to an optical characterization setup. We are currently limited to optical measurements of the OSL molecules in solution. However, material properties are ultimately most important in the solid state (e.g., as a thin-film device). To that end and for a different scientific goal, we are developing a self-driving lab for inorganic thin-film materials focused on the oxygen evolution reaction. While the future of self-driving laboratories is very promising, numerous challenges still need to be overcome. These challenges can be split into cognition and motor function. Generally, the cognitive challenges are related to optimization with constraints or unexpected outcomes for which general algorithmic solutions have yet to be developed. A more practical challenge that could be resolved in the near future is that of software control and integration because few instrument manufacturers design their products with self-driving laboratories in mind. Challenges in motor function are largely related to handling heterogeneous systems, such as dispensing solids or performing extractions. As a result, it is critical to understand that adapting experimental procedures that were designed for human experimenters is not as simple as transferring those same actions to an automated system, and there may be more efficient ways to achieve the same goal in an automated fashion. Accordingly, for self-driving laboratories, we need to carefully rethink the translation of manual experimental protocols.

Abstract:
Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) promise an accelerated application of the scientific method. Through the automation of experimental workflows, along with autonomous experimental planning, SDLs hold the potential to greatly accelerate research in chemistry and materials discovery. This review provides an in-depth analysis of the state-of-the-art in SDL technology, its applications across various scientific disciplines, and the potential implications for research and industry. This review additionally provides an overview of the enabling technologies for SDLs, including their hardware, software, and integration with laboratory infrastructure. Most importantly, this review explores the diverse range of scientific domains where SDLs have made significant contributions, from drug discovery and materials science to genomics and chemistry. We provide a comprehensive review of existing real-world examples of SDLs, their different levels of automation, and the challenges and limitations associated with each domain.

    author = "Pelkie, Brenden and Baird, Sterling and Aissi, Eunice and Aspuru-Takata, Kenzo and Cao, Yang and Chang, Jin Hyun and Gambhir, Kshitij and Hale, Wm Salt and Hao, Lucy and Hattrick, Chance and Hein, Jason and Luo, Danli and Melville, Owen and Ngan, Monique and Nyeland, Louie Lucas Bisgaard and Peek, Nadya and Politi, Maria and Rajkumar, Ethan Elliot and Siemenn, Alexander and Subbaraman, Blair and Vasquez, Sonya and Watchorn, Jeffrey and Zhang, Wenyu and Ziskason, Rógvi and Pozzo, Lilo and Buonassisi, Tonio and Vegge, Tejs",

Abstract:
<jats:p>As self-driving labs become widely deployed in chemicals and materials research, interest in democratizing access to these platforms is growing. SDLs can be democratized by lowering their costs to increase accessibility and by creating systems that allow researchers to modify, extend, and share SDL tools to meet the needs of their science and contribute to the advancement of the SDL community. In particular, user-developed automation infrastructure is an important component of democratized SDL ecosystems. To advance community adoption of democratized SDL practices, we organized the “Democratizing Self-Driving Labs” workshop held at the 2024 Accelerate conference. As part of this workshop, the authors contributed to a demonstration of their user-developed automation infrastructure. 14 examples of custom built hardware, software, and workflows were shared. This workshop provided an opportunity for researchers to see user-developed infrastructure in action, learn about how they could integrate these projects into their work, and contribute to discussion about what is needed to advance the state of democratized SDLs. In this perspective, ten contributed examples of user-developed hardware and software from the workshop are highlighted. Despite a diverse array of projects, common motivations for pursuing user-developed infrastructure were cost savings and specification requirements that were unmet by commercially available products. Continuing to advance the state of user-developed automation infrastructure will require commitments to completing high-quality documentation for open-source hardware projects, pathways for materials researchers to learn hardware development skills, and more opportunities for researchers to share their infrastructure advancements, in addition to the scientific advancements that they enable.</jats:p>

## 7. Review of Low-cost Self-driving Laboratories: The "Frugal Twin" Concept

    title = {Review of Low-cost Self-driving Laboratories: The "Frugal Twin" Concept},
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ChemRxiv",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.26434/chemrxiv-2023-6z9mq",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2023-6z9mq",
    publisher = "American Chemical Society (ACS)"
}


Abstract:
<jats:p>This review proposes the concept of a “frugal twin,” similar to a digital twin, but for physical experiments. Frugal twins range from simple toy examples to low-cost surrogates of high-cost research. For example, a color-mixing self-driving laboratory (SDL) is a low-cost version of a costly multi-step chemical discovery SDL. We need frugal twins because they provide hands-on experience, a test bed for software prototyping (e.g., optimization, data infrastructure), and a low barrier to entry for democratizing SDLs. However, there is room for improvement. The true value of frugal twins can be realized in three core areas. Firstly, hardware and software modularity, secondly, purpose-built design (human-inspired vs. hardware-centric vs. human-in-the-loop), and thirdly state-of-the-art (SOTA) software (e.g., multi-fidelity optimization). We also describe the ethical benefits and risks that come with the democratization of science through frugal twins. In future work, we suggest ideas for new frugal twins, SDL educational course outcomes, and a classification scheme for autonomy levels.</jats:p>

## 11. Self-Driving Laboratories for Development of New Functional Materials and Optimizing Known Reactions

'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 1-2', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 2-4', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 4-6', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 6-7', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 7-9', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 9-10', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 10-11', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 11-13', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 13-15', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 15-16', 'soldatov2021selfdrivinglaboratoriesfor pages 16-16'

    title = "Self-Driving Laboratories for Development of New Functional Materials and Optimizing Known Reactions",

    issn = "2079-4991"
}


Abstract:
Innovations often play an essential role in the acceleration of the new functional materials discovery. The success and applicability of the synthesis results with new chemical compounds and materials largely depend on the previous experience of the researcher himself and the modernity of the equipment used in the laboratory. Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies are the next step in developing the solution for practical problems in science, including the development of new materials. Those technologies go broadly beyond the borders of a computer science branch and give new insights and practical possibilities within the far areas of expertise and chemistry applications. One of the attractive challenges is an automated new functional material synthesis driven by AI. However, while having many years of hands-on experience, chemistry specialists have a vague picture of AI. To strengthen and underline AI’s role in materials discovery, a short introduction is given to the essential technologies, and the machine learning process is explained. After this review, this review summarizes the recent studies of new strategies that help automate and accelerate the development of new functional materials. ## 12. Self-Driving Laboratories: Translating Materials Science from Laboratory to Factory

Abstract:
The field of materials science stands at a critical inflection point. While laboratory innovations continue to emerge at an unprecedented pace, the traditional timeline from discovery to market in 10–20 years has become an unacceptable bottleneck in addressing urgent technological challenges. We argue that self-driving laboratories (SDLs) represent not merely another step in automation, but a fundamental reimagining of the materials development pipeline. By integrating manufacturing constraints and scalability considerations from the earliest stages of discovery, SDLs can collapse the laboratory-to-factory timeline while improving reproducibility and success rates. This requires abandoning the traditional sequential approach of materials screening, device optimization and manufacturing scale-up; in favor of concurrent cross-scale development. Here, we critically examine current SDL implementations, challenge prevailing assumptions about automation in materials science, and propose a roadmap for truly integrated materials development platforms that could revolutionize how we translate laboratory discoveries into commercial products.

Abstract:
<jats:p>The past decade has witnessed remarkable advancements in autonomous systems, such as automobiles that are evolving from traditional vehicles to ones capable of navigating complex environments without human intervention. Similarly, the rise of self-driving laboratories, which leverage robotics and artificial intelligence to accelerate discovery, is driving a paradigm shift in scientific research. As SDLs evolve to expand the scope of chemical processes that can be performed, it is essential to bring safety to the forefront of SDL research to ensure necessary safeguards against potential accidents that range from near-misses to catastrophic failures. This perspective examines the trajectory of self-driving laboratories, juxtaposing their development with those of other autonomous technologies, with a particular focus on safety. We explore current safety status and concerns, identify opportunities for innovation to shape this rapidly evolving landscape, and reflect on the actions the SDL community can take moving forward.</jats:p>

## 1. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders

    author = "Zegzulka, Jiri and Gelnar, Daniel and Jezerska, Lucie and Prokes, Rostislav and Rozbroj, Jiri",
    title = "Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "Scientific Reports",

    issn = "2045-2322"
}


Abstract:
With the rise of additive technologies, the characterization of metal powders is increasingly required. There is a need to precisely match the properties of metal powders to a specific machine and to ensure highly consistent production. Therefore, the study aims at a detailed characterization of ten metal powders (Metal powder 316 L, Zn, Sn, Al, Cu, Mn, Fe, Bronze, Ti and Mo powder), for which the particle size distribution, morphology, static and dynamic angle of repose and the effective internal friction angle (AIFE) were determined. The AIFE parameter and flow index were determined from three commonly used rotary shear devices: The computer-controlled Ring Shear Tester RST-01. pc, the Brookfield PFT Powder Flow Tester and the FT4 Powder rheometer. The results showed that the values for the device of one manufacturer did not fully correspond to the values of another one. The flow characteristics of the metal powders were quantified from the particle size distribution data, static angle of repose, and AIFE data. According to the particle size distribution and angle of repose (AOR), 50% of the tested metal powders fell into the free-flowing mode. According to the evaluation of AIFE, 20% of the samples fell into the lower area. Based on the flow indexes calculated from the measurements of the shear devices used, 100% (RST-01.pc), 70% (PFT) and 50% (FT4) of the samples were included in the free-flowing category. When comparing the results, attention should be paid not only to the nature of the material, but also to the methodology and equipment used. A comparison of methodologies revealed similarities in the changing behavior of the different metal powders. A comparison of effective angles of AIFE and static AOR was shown, and a hypothesis of the conversion relation was derived.

Abstract:
Metal powders are key to metal additive manufacturing technologies such as powder bed fusion. These powder feedstocks experience a range of forces and physical phenomena both during the powder bed fusion process and additional post-processing stages that can alter their composition and material properties. To evaluate such effects, these powders need to be characterized, tested, and analyzed at critical stages of their lifecycles. This paper provides a review of the current state of the art for powder evaluation methods and their applicability for powder bed fusion production. Methods are categorized by the properties they evaluate, either particulate or bulk properties. Industry standards are identified for each method if applicable and the advantages and disadvantages of each are defined. Effects of these properties on the flowability and spreadability of powders are synthesized and practical management guidelines are defined. This paper aims at providing an overview of powder evaluation for powder bed fusion, practical considerations for the development of powder test and evaluation programs, and provide insights for future research undertakings in the field.

Abstract:
The perpetual significance of the pharmaceutical industry in society necessitates ongoing research efforts to enhance the efficacy of its manufacturing processes. Given that drug product manufacturing typically involves powder processing, a thorough understanding of powder characterization is needed for optimal process performance. Powder rheology is commonly examined in pharmaceutical manufacturing to elucidate the relationship between powder properties and the performance of pharmaceutical processes. This paper provides a brief discussion of recent literature regarding the various powder properties and characterization techniques encompassed in powder rheology. The powder properties are categorized into particle size, particle morphology, friability, electrostatics, permeability, wettability, cohesion, bulk density, and agglomeration sections. A distinct focus is placed on the segment about powder wettability. This review informs readers about the fundamental properties of powders known to influence pharmaceutical processes. It discusses the interrelationships among these properties, powder characterization techniques, and ideal states of powder properties that lead to optimal process performance.

Abstract:
Preformulation studies like storage, transfer, fluidisation, and compaction, are subject to the excellent 
 flowability of the powder. It is often required for proper operational management and consistent design 
 of industrial processes. Two basics methods have been utilised to characterise the flow behaviour of bulk 
 solids. Traditionally, powder has been evaluated in terms of simple and straight forward techniques like 
 Hausner’s ratio, Carr’s index, bulk density, and angle of repose. Despite that, the results obtained in the 
 traditional method lack predictability, reproducibility, sensitivity, and the actual association between the 
 resulting data and flow behaviour. Henceforth the results varied for weight variation, content uniformity, 
 and dissolution, etc. during the preparation of final dosage forms. To overcome these limitations of the 
 traditional methods, the advanced techniques are being utilised to explore micro properties like the angle 
 of internal friction, bulk density, flow function, yield loci, powder rheometry under precisely controlled 
 conditions of consolidation stress. Ultimately these techniques help to design, choose suitable excipients, 
 reduce time along with cost, and improve the health of society. This review focuses on some of the most 
 commonly applied methods used in traditional and advanced shear cell testing techniques to measure the 
 flow of powders.

Abstract:
Η αδυναμία των συνοχικών κόνεων να ρέουν σταθερά και αξιόπιστα αποτελεί κύρια αιτία διακοπής λειτουργίας και απώλειας προϊόντων σε ένα ευρύ φάσμα βιομηχανιών επεξεργασίας σκονών. Έχει πραγματοποιηθεί εκτεταμένη έρευνα για τον χαρακτηρισμό της ρευστότητας των σκονών χρησιμοποιώντας μια ευρεία γκάμα τεχνικών, με την πιο εδραιωμένη μέθοδο μέτρησης της ρευστότητας σκονών σε μέτριες έως υψηλές τάσεις να είναι τα κυψελοειδή εργαλεία διάτμησης, με θεωρίες που έχουν αναπτυχθεί για τον σχεδιασμό σιλό και χοανών. Ωστόσο, πολλές βιομηχανικές διαδικασίες μεγάλου ενδιαφέροντος, όπως η πλήρωση και δοσομέτρηση σκονών σε κάψουλες και η διασπορά σε εισπνευστήρες ξηρών σκονών (DPI), εκθέτουν τα σωματίδια σε πολύ χαμηλές τάσεις συμπύκνωσης (≤ 1 kPa), όπου ο προσδιορισμός της ασυγκράτητης αντοχής διάρρηξης μέσω δοκιμών διάτμησης συχνά παρουσιάζει ασυνέπειες στη μέτρηση ή σε σύγκριση με την παρατηρούμενη συμπεριφορά. Επομένως, υπάρχει ανάγκη για μια εναλλακτική προσέγγιση για την κατανόηση της ρευστότητας των ασθενώς συμπυκνωμένων σκονών, όπως η μέθοδος εμβάπτισης με μπάλα, η οποία είναι μια τεχνική διείσδυσης που μπορεί να αξιολογήσει τη ρευστότητα των σκονών μέχρι πολύ χαμηλές τάσεις, ενώ απαιτεί μόνο μια πολύ μικρή ποσότητα υλικού. Σε αυτήν την τεχνική, ένα κρεβάτι σκόνης συμπυκνώνεται σε οποιαδήποτε επιθυμητή τάση που παρέχει μια σχετικά επίπεδη επιφάνεια και στη συνέχεια διεισδύεται από έναν σφαιρικό διαβρωτή. Η ρευστότητα προσδιορίζεται με τον υπολογισμό της σκληρότητας του κρεβατιού σκόνης, από την απόκριση δύναμης-μετατόπισης του κρεβατιού. Η σκληρότητα μπορεί να συσχετιστεί με την ασυγκράτητη αντοχή διάρρηξης, που συνήθως προκύπτει μέσω των δοκιμών διάτμησης, μέσω του συντελεστή περιορισμού, ο οποίος εξαρτάται από τις ιδιότητες των σωματιδίων, αν και δεν μπορεί ακόμα να προσδιοριστεί εκ των προτέρων. Σε αυτήν την εργασία, ο συντελεστής περιορισμού μιας ευρείας κλάσης σκονών ποσοτικοποιήθηκε μέσω πειραμάτων εμβάπτισης και κυψελών διάτμησης σε μέτριες έως υψηλές τάσεις και διαπιστώθηκε ότι γενικά είναι ανεξάρτητος από την εφαρμοζόμενη τάση, ενώ για τέσσερα από τα είκοσι πέντε υλικά παρουσίασε διακυμάνσεις. Για να εξαχθεί η ασυγκράτητη αντοχή διάρρηξης από τις μετρήσεις σκληρότητας σε χαμηλές τάσεις, υποτέθηκε ότι ο συντελεστής περιορισμού παραμένει σταθερός σε χαμηλότερες τάσεις. Χρησιμοποιήθηκε επίσης η Μοντελοποίηση Διακριτών Στοιχείων (DEM) για τη προσομοίωση του συστήματος εμβάπτισης με μπάλα, επιτρέποντας τον προσδιορισμό των εσωτερικών τάσεων διάρρηξης του κρεβατιού σκόνης προκειμένου να διασαφηνιστεί η συμπεριφορά του συντελεστή περιορισμού σε χαμηλές τάσεις. Οι προσομοιώσεις επιβεβαίωσαν την υπόθεση ότι ο συντελεστής περιορισμού παραμένει σταθερός καθ' όλη τη διάρκεια του φάσματος εφαρμοζόμενων τάσεων.Επιπλέον, η εφαρμοσιμότητα και αξιοπιστία τόσο της εμβάπτισης με μπάλα όσο και της κυψέλης διάτμησης FT4 αξιολογήθηκαν σε ένα ευρύ φάσμα τόσο χαμηλών όσο και μέτριων έως υψηλών τάσεων. Η εμβάπτιση με μπάλα έδωσε πολύ επαναλήψιμα αποτελέσματα σε ολόκληρο το φάσμα των εφαρμοζόμενων τάσεων, ενώ η κυψέλη διάτμησης FT4 κρίθηκε αναξιόπιστη για τα περισσότερα υλικά σε κανονικές τάσεις προδιάτμησης 1 kPa και κάτω. Για όλα τα υλικά, εκτός από τις τρεις σκόνες που παραμένουν πολύ συνοχικές καθ' όλη τη διάρκεια του φάσματος τάσεων που δοκιμάστηκε, παρατηρήθηκε ότι η αύξηση της σκληρότητας (και συνεπώς και της ασυγκράτητης αντοχής διάρρηξης που προκύπτει από την εμβάπτιση με μπάλα) με την τάση ήταν πολύ πιο απότομη σε χαμηλές τάσεις, σε σύγκριση με υψηλότερες τάσεις, λόγω της πιο γρήγορης αύξησης της πυκνότητας συσκευασίας. Για όλα τα μοντέλα γυάλινων σφαιριδίων που δοκιμάστηκαν, εκτός από τα δείγματα 0 - 20 μm, η σκληρότητα βρέθηκε να είναι ανεξάρτητη από το βάθος διείσδυσης σε ένα συγκεκριμένο εύρος βάθους. Αντίθετα, για τα περισσότερα 'πραγματικά' υλικά, καθώς και για τα προαναφερθέντα πολύ λεπτά γυάλινα σφαιρίδια μοντέλου, η σκληρότητα βρέθηκε να αυξάνεται συνεχώς με το βάθος, με μια κλίση που είναι ανεξάρτητη από την εφαρμοζόμενη τάση και παρόμοια για όλα τα υλικά που δοκιμάστηκαν. Οι σκόνες που είναι επιρρεπείς σε ασταθή ροή (stick-slip) αποκλίνουν από την παραπάνω συμπεριφορά και εμφάνισαν μια κυμαινόμενη απόκριση δύναμης. Η επίδραση μιας ποικιλίας ιδιοτήτων σωματιδίων στον συντελεστή περιορισμού και συνεπώς στη ρευστότητα της σκόνης προσδιορίστηκε τόσο πειραματικά όσο και υπολογιστικά. Οι επιδράσεις του μεγέθους των σωματιδίων, της κατανομής του μεγέθους, και του σχήματος μεμονωμένων σωματιδίων και συσσωματωμάτων μελετήθηκαν πειραματικά, ενώ η στατική και κυλιόμενη τριβή, καθώς και η πλαστική τάση διάρρηξης μεταβλήθηκαν στις προσομοιώσεις DEM. Η επίδραση της επιφανειακής ενέργειας μελετήθηκε τόσο πειραματικά όσο και με τη μοντελοποίηση DEM. Όλες οι ιδιότητες βρέθηκαν να επηρεάζουν τον συντελεστή περιορισμού και τη συμπεριφορά ροής σε κάποιο βαθμό, υποδεικνύοντας την πολυπλοκότητα της συμπεριφοράς ροής των σκονών.

    issue = "5",

}


Abstract:
Additive manufacturing is a rapidly expanding field, encompassing many methods to manufacture parts and coatings with a wide variety of feedstock. Metal powders are one such feedstock, with a range of compositions and morphologies. Understanding subtle changes in the feedstock is critical to ensure successful consolidation and quality control of both the feedstock and manufactured part. Current standards lack the ability to finely distinguish almost acceptable powders from barely acceptable ones. Here, novel means of powder feedstock characterization for quality control are demonstrated for the solid-state AM process of cold spray, though similar methods may be extrapolated to other additive methods as well. These characterization methods aim to capture the physics of the process, which in cold spray consists of high strain rate deformation of solid-state feedstock. To capture this, in this effort powder compaction was evaluated via rapidly applied loads, flowability of otherwise non-flowable powders was evaluated with the addition of vibration, and powder electrical resistivity was evaluated through compaction between two electrodes. Several powders, including aluminum alloys, chromium, and cermet composites, were evaluated in this effort, with each case study demonstrating the need for non-traditional characterization metrics as a means of quality control and classification of these materials.

Abstract:
Spray drying as a particle engineering technique is of increasing interest in the field of inhalation and is already being utilised e.g., for the PulmoSphereTM products. As spray dried particles tend to agglomerate and are mechanically instable, low dose filling processes can be difficult. This study correlates powder flowability tests of spray dried formulations with filling processes with drum and dosator systems. Four pulmonary and four nasal powders with different characteristics in terms of shape, composition, and surface polarity were prepared and characterised for powder flowability according to Ph. Eur. and by powder rheometry. All formulations were filled with a manual drum TT and a dosator system. The classical flowability tests according to the Ph. Eur. showed a bad flow behaviour for hydrophilic pulmonary powders (x50 ~ 3 µm), whereas hydrophobic pulmonary particles and nasal particles (x50 ~ 25 µm) showed a better flowing behaviour. Powder rheometry supports this finding but can better differentiate flow behaviours.

## 1. Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder
Valid Text Names:
'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 1-2', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 3-4', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 4-5', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 5-7', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 7-11', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 11-12', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 12-13', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 13-13'

    author = "Fathollahi, Sara and Sacher, Stephan and Escotet-Espinoza, M. Sebastian and DiNunzio, James and Khinast, Johannes G.",
    title = "Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "AAPS PharmSciTech",
    volume = "21",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "8",
    issn = "1530-9932"
}


Abstract:
Highly potent active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and low-dose excipients, or excipients with very low density, are notoriously hard to feed with currently available commercial technology. The micro-feeder system presented in this work is capable of feeding low-dose rates of powders with different particle sizes and flow properties. Two different grades of lactose, di-calcium phosphate, croscarmellose sodium, silicon dioxide, a spray-dried intermediate, and an active ingredient were studied to vary material properties to test performance of the system. The current micro-feeder system is a volumetric feeder combined with a weighing balance at the outlet that measures feeder output rates. Feeding results are shown as a so-called “displacement-feed factor” curve for each material. Since the powder mass and volume are known in the micro-feeder system, in this work, we characterized an observed density variation during processing via a “displacement-feed factor” profile for each of the fed powders. This curve can be later used for calibrating the system to ensure an accurate, constant feed rate and in addition predicting feeding performance for that material at any feed rate. There is a relation between powder properties and feeding performance. Powders with finer particles and higher compressibility show densification during their feeding process. However, powders with larger particles and lower compressibility show both “densification” and “powder bed expansion,” which is the manifestation of dilation and elastic recovery of particles during the micro-feeding process. Through the application of the displacement-feed factor, it is possible to provide precise feeding accuracy of low-dose materials.

## 6. Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system
Valid Text Names:
'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 1-2', 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 2-5', 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 5-7', 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 7-9', 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 9-10', 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 10-10'

BibTex:
@article{pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation,
    author = "Pu, Hua-Yan and Xie, Rong-Qing and Peng, Yan and Yang, Yang and He, Shi-Yang and Luo, Jun and Sun, Yi and Xie, Shao-Rong and Luo, Jun",
    title = "Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system",
    year = "2019",
    journal = "Advances in Manufacturing",
    volume = "7",
    pages = "278-287",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "3",
    issn = "2095-3127"
}


Abstract:
In recent years, the development of techniques for the controlled preparation of functional graded materials (FGMs) has become a vigorous research field. In this study, to improve the efficiency and accuracy of sample preparations, an automated feeding system based on gravimetric principles for dry powder with three dosing feeders is designed and realized. The feeding rate and accuracy can be regulated by coordinating the protruded length L (mm) and rotational speed V (r/min) of the feeder stirrer. To demonstrate this automatic sample preparation system, the well-known thermoelectric material BixSb2−xTe3 (x = 0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.7 and 0.8) is selected and prepared by the developed system, and the composition distribution of the functional graded material is characterized. Experimental results show that the BixSb2−xTe3 (x = 0.3–0.8) functionally graded material crystalizes in the rhombohedral phase after hot-pressing sintering and annealing and the prepared sample has a good gradient composition distribution. This verifies the reliability and accuracy of the feeding system. The concept of samples with a gradient component and application of the automatic powder feeding system could considerably accelerate the research and development of new materials.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. High-Throughput Phase Screening and Laser-Directed Energy Deposition of Ti-Ni-Nb Gradient Alloys
Valid Text Names:
'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 1-2', 'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 2-5', 'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 5-8', 'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 8-9', 'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 9-10'

BibTex:
@article{li2025highthroughputphasescreening,
    author = "Li, Jinlong and Zhang, Xiaowei and An, Zhe and Li, Biqiang and Wang, Yizheng and Yang, Yaoyuan and Tong, Kexin and Zhu, Yingze",
    title = "High-Throughput Phase Screening and Laser-Directed Energy Deposition of Ti-Ni-Nb Gradient Alloys",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "Coatings",
    volume = "15",
    pages = "401",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.3390/coatings15040401",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/coatings15040401",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "4",
    issn = "2079-6412"
}


Abstract:
This work presents an integrated directed energy deposition (DED) approach utilizing a multi-powder feeder with real-time continuously variable composition functionality, a multi-powder mixer and a multi-powder nozzle to fabricate Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys with controlled compositional variations. The high-throughput methodology enables rapid alloy design and optimization by allowing precise manipulation of chemical composition and phase structures within a single deposited track. The EDS analysis confirms a gradual increase in titanium content, a nearly constant nickel content and a decrease in niobium along the scanning path, aligning with the expected powder-feeding trends. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis further reveals a phase transition from niobium-rich intermetallic compounds (NbNi4, Nb8Ni) at the beginning of the deposition to titanium-rich phases (Ti, Ti2Ni) at the end, demonstrating the ability to tailor phase distributions through real-time composition control. This high-throughput methodology enables rapid alloy design and optimization by integrating theoretical predictions with experimental phase screening. This study establishes a novel framework for the rapid discovery and optimization of functionally graded materials, paving the way for advanced applications in aerospace, biomedical implants and high-performance structural components.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the key details about powder deposition devices, multi-material powder dispensing mechanisms, and powder feeding systems for multi-material additive manufacturing? What specific devices, resolutions, and accuracies are described?", "text_name_focus_list": ["schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 5-7", "schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 7-8", "schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 8-10", "mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 4-5", "mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 5-7", "mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 7-9"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the key methods, powder feeders, and approaches for high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery using directed energy deposition with compositional gradients? What powder feeding systems are used for mixing multiple elemental powders?", "text_name_focus_list": ["vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 1-4", "vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 4-7", "li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 1-2", "li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 2-4", "pegues2021exploringadditivemanufacturing pages 1-5", "xie2025anoverviewof pages 3-4"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 5-7'.

M. Schneck, M. Horn, Matthias Schmitt, C. Seidel, G. Schlick, and G. Reinhart. Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. Progress in Additive Manufacturing, 6:881-894, Aug 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2. This article has 132 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes two main classes of multi-material powder deposition devices: nozzle-based dispensers and masked-deposition concepts (photoconductor and pressure-based). Nozzle systems use actuators to fluidize powder and print single powder lines; some extrude metal-powder pastes and some use additional suction nozzles or holohedral coaters. Reported nozzle feed/deposition rates include 10–30 mg/s (5–15 mm/s travel) for copper and SnPb, up to 32 mg/s for 316L, 10–13 mg/s for copper/H13, and 19.7–64.2 mg/s across other materials; estimated volumetric rates are 0.4–4.1 mm3/s per nozzle. Achieved powder feature/track sizes include 85 µm, 555 µm, and 1.3 mm (track heights 100–150 µm); part features reported: 50 µm nominal layer height, 1 mm cross-sections, 150 µm lattice struts, and circuit paths ~2 mm × 0.2 mm (with defects). Nozzle approaches are at MRL 4–5. Masked concepts: photoconductor (xerography-like) demonstrated in lab (MRL 2) with ~1 mm powder features but no deposition rates; pressure-based sieve-drum modules can suction and deposit patterned layers — a demo created 150 mm length in ~8 s, giving an estimated 130–290 mm3/s and single line widths of 0.8–1.6 mm; pressure-based maturity MRL 3–4. The excerpt notes minimum feature sizes of solidified materials and quantitative accuracy metrics have not yet been studied.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'zhang2019laserbasedadditive pages 46-49'.

X Zhang. Laser based additive manufacturing of multiple material metal and glass objects. Unknown journal, 2019.

The excerpt discusses powder deposition approaches and powder-feeding devices for multi-material selective laser/multi-material powder-bed AM (MMSLM). Powder beds are preferred for multi-material layer support; the primary goal is discrete powder deposition with high accuracy and efficiency. Powder classification (Geldart/Deldart) guides method choice: Group B (metals) are sand-like and gravity-flowable; Group A (30–100 µm) are aeratable and need pneumatic or acoustic aid; Group C (10–30 µm) are cohesive. Devices/mechanisms described include: pneumatic aspirating-dispensing heads (volumetric aspiration/injection) — enable multi-material dosing but have low time-efficiency and lack continuous metering; plunger-based volumetric dosing — similar limitations and deposits separated doses; electrostatic/3D xerography — layered xerographic transfer/compaction, noted as possibly having the highest resolution but unsuitable for dielectric powders; gas pressure-assisted delivery using L-valves — provides on/off and modulated flow but suffers deposition accuracy loss from blown-off powder; vibration/ultrasonic capillary feeders with piezoelectric actuators — dispense from micro-scale capillaries giving high-resolution deposition and good on/off and flow modulation control, but are slow due to small capillary diameters. Vibration methods are highlighted as most suitable overall for MMSLM in the text.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'schneck2021reviewonadditive pages 4-5'.

M. Schneck, M. Horn, Matthias Schmitt, C. Seidel, G. Schlick, and G. Reinhart. Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. Progress in Additive Manufacturing, 6:881-894, Aug 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2. This article has 132 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt categorizes multi-material powder deposition for PBF into three device classes: holohedral (coat entire bed), nozzle-based (localized deposition), and masked deposition concepts. Holohedral coaters achieve MM capability by separating powder storage/delivery chambers within the coater and can spread materials evenly across the build plate; they have been demonstrated at MRL ~4–5. A practical holohedral hybrid approach (Anstaett et al.) uses a suction unit to remove unsolidified powder and requires four coater travels per layer (deposit A, suction A, deposit B, suction B). Estimated deposition rates for that system are 60–140 mm3/s versus 240–560 mm3/s for mono-material PBF-LB. Typical effective layer heights cited are 75–110 µm. Feature resolution is laser-limited and comparable to mono-material PBF-LB, with minimum feature sizes ~130–240 µm and nominal overlap zones of 100–200 µm at 30 µm layer height; sub-millimeter features (<<1×1×1 mm3) have been demonstrated for some alloys. Nozzle-based concepts, present since the 1990s, must address powder flow issues (interparticular forces) via actuators that fluidize powders for defined deposition.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 7-9'.

Mehrshad Mehrpouya, Daniel Tuma, Tom Vaneker, Mohamadreza Afrasiabi, Markus Bambach, and Ian Gibson. Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques. Rapid Prototyping Journal, 28:1-19, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014, doi:10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014. This article has 133 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes multiple powder deposition and feeding approaches used for multimaterial powder-bed fusion. Demir and Previtali’s “Powderful” multimaterial SLM prototype uses a double powder feeder with two hoppers (distinct powders on top) and a mixing system on the bottom to mix two metallic powders on-demand, allowing per-layer composition control and creating multimaterial surfaces in a single layer. Other SLM builds used two external powder feeding systems controlled by a powder flow regulator to switch/combine feedstocks. An Aconity SLM used an Aerosint powder deposition head for multimaterial deposition (316L/CuCrZr example). For metal–polymer multimaterial LPBF, a polymer layer is deposited by an ultrasonic vibration-assisted nozzle (UVAN), which includes a powder-removing nozzle to clear residues, followed by a pneumatic powder-leveling blade; the laser then selectively fuses the laid powders. Reported challenges include polymer melting/contamination during metal scanning and carbon residue on Cu10Sn causing balling; interfaces can be diffused or crack-prone (e.g., copper diffusing into steel). The excerpt outlines device types and mechanisms but does not provide numeric resolutions or stated positional/dispensing accuracies.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'zhang2019laserbasedadditive pages 35-40'.

X Zhang. Laser based additive manufacturing of multiple material metal and glass objects. Unknown journal, 2019.

The excerpt describes several powder deposition and multi-material dispensing approaches relevant to multi-material AM. Powder-bed fusion (PBF) typically deposits powder layers with a scraper or roller inside an inert (nitrogen) chamber, but that conventional deposition cannot easily support multi-material beds. One experimental modification added two cylindrical bores to an SLS machine to feed copper and silver powders and produce vertically graded multi-material samples. Example powder/feed parameters: silica powder <20 µm, layer thickness 100 µm. Nozzle- or jet-based approaches enable multi-material deposition via multiple print heads (Pa et al. used two heads to deposit conductive and dielectric materials). Material jetting produces droplets (1–10 µm drop size) using thermal/electrostatic/piezoelectric/acoustic actuation; the nScrypt 3Dn-300 nozzle is cited as having very high deposition accuracy and achieving line widths as narrow as 15 µm. The text notes challenges integrating material jetting with laser-based PBF due to mismatched resolutions and low throughput.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'zhang2019laserbasedadditive pages 40-46'.

X Zhang. Laser based additive manufacturing of multiple material metal and glass objects. Unknown journal, 2019.

The excerpt describes several powder-deposition devices and mechanisms used in multi-material powder-bed AM. Common single-material PBF used scrapers or rollers, but these cause cross-contamination and are unsuitable for multi-material work. Early multi-material hardware included adding dedicated powder bores (e.g., two cylindrical bores for copper and silver in an SLS machine) to enable vertically graded two-material deposition. Discrete in-layer multi-material approaches include the University of Texas MMSLS method that combined scraping and selective vacuum suction to deposit two dry powders in a single layer; however, roller-based deposition caused contamination and the manually operated vacuum suction produced very low accuracy. Later MMSLM systems (Al-Jamal, Chao Wei et al.) used combinations of scraping deposition, suction removal, nozzle dispensing, and laser processing (Nd:YAG via fibre optic) to produce multi-metal parts. Reported problems include uncompressed powder deposition, resulting porosity and cracking. The excerpt describes device types and mechanisms (rollers/scrapers, suction, bores, nozzle dispensing, fibre-laser processing) and qualitative accuracy/contamination issues but does not provide numeric resolutions or specific quantitative accuracy metrics.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'mehrpouya2022multimaterialpowderbed pages 5-7'.

Mehrshad Mehrpouya, Daniel Tuma, Tom Vaneker, Mohamadreza Afrasiabi, Markus Bambach, and Ian Gibson. Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques. Rapid Prototyping Journal, 28:1-19, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014, doi:10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014. This article has 133 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes several powder-deposition and multi-material dispensing approaches used in multimaterial powder-bed SLS. Aerosint SA's commercial system uses a recoater made of patterning drums (each drum holding a distinct powder) and a dedicated powder-deposition head to selectively lay different powder voxels layer-by-layer across the build area; the company positions this as a low-waste method compatible with ceramics, metals and polymers and enabling local optimization of mechanical and functional properties. An alternative reported method is electrophotographic powder transfer (Stichel et al., 2018) using a two-chamber transfer setup for multimaterial deposition. Whitehead and Lipson (2020) implemented an inverted-laser workflow that coats powders onto clear glass plates; the laser is pointed upward through the plates to selectively sinter, then the selectively bonded layer is lifted and moved to a differently coated plate, enabling multimaterial stacking without a large powder bed. The excerpt gives an example sample (50 layers at 2.18 mm each) but does not provide quantitative resolution, dimensional tolerance, feed-rate, or positional-accuracy numbers for the deposition heads or powder-feeding systems.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'zhang2019laserbasedadditive pages 55-62'.

X Zhang. Laser based additive manufacturing of multiple material metal and glass objects. Unknown journal, 2019.

The excerpt describes vibratory capillary/nozzle-based powder dispensing used for multi-material powder delivery. Key geometric rules: nozzle inner diameter should be at least ~5× the particle (grain) size and R/r ratios (capillary radius to particle radius) control jamming, dome formation and overflow; a commonly cited threshold is R/r>5. Experimental nozzle diameters referenced range from ~150 µm to 360 µm, with flowrates showing slow increase 150→240 µm and a steeper rise 240→360 µm. Orifice angle strongly affects feeding: <30°, 30°–60°, and >60° were compared, and 30°–60° gave the most stable feeding. Powder feeding is vibrationally driven: discharge has a critical condition controlled by vibrational acceleration a(2πf)2 (acceleration depends on power, amplitude and frequency) and an optimum acceleration yields highest flowrate. Trade-offs: larger R/r and higher vibrational acceleration increase overflow mass (deposited after vibration stops), so flowrate and deposition resolution conflict. Line geometry (resolution) depends on orifice height, scan speed and powder delivery rate (examples: orifice heights 0.3–0.9 mm; scan speeds 10–50 mm/s; powder delivery rates ~5–17 mg/s). The excerpt gives dimensional and operational guidance but no specific commercial device names or explicit accuracy/resolution tolerances beyond these geometric, flowrate, and speed figures.


Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: []

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007]

messages.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000008

From text 'liu2024high‐throughputpreparationfor pages 12-12'.

Min Liu, Chenxu Lei, Yongxiang Wang, Baicheng Zhang, and Xuan-hui Qu. High‐throughput preparation for alloy composition design in additive manufacturing: a comprehensive review. Materials Genome Engineering Advances, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1002/mgea.55, doi:10.1002/mgea.55. This article has 35 citations.

The excerpt describes high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery implemented mainly with laser directed energy deposition (DED) and laser powder bed fusion (LPBF). In DED, multiple nozzles deliver elemental or pre-alloyed powders to a laser-created micro-melt pool and the powders are alloyed in situ; process parameters (laser energy, scan speed, trajectory) tune composition and microstructure. Common powder feeding approaches for producing compositional gradients include multi-hopper systems (each hopper holding different powder proportions) where each powder is printed for a set number of layers and composition is changed by switching hoppers (Z-axis gradients). An alternative high-efficiency approach is gradient powder spreading, which places different powders in distinct zones within a hopper and shifts the gradient direction to horizontal; powder storage, mixing, and layer-by-layer spreading produce a controlled, consistent ratio of powders per layer. The text notes practical limits: some DED implementations lack precision in composition control and struggle to fabricate continuous gradients, while gradient-spreading schemes aim to address these issues. Examples cited include Zhang et al. (Fe-Cr-Ni, 80 compositions) and Nie et al. (SS316L-SS431 gradients).


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'nelaturu2024multiprincipalelementalloy pages 1-4'.

Phalgun Nelaturu, Jason R. Hattrick-Simpers, Michael Moorehead, Vrishank Jambur, Izabela Szlufarska, Adrien Couet, and Dan J. Thoma. Multi-principal element alloy discovery using directed energy deposition and machine learning. Materials Science and Engineering: A, 891:145945, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945. This article has 40 citations.

The authors demonstrate a high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery workflow using in situ alloying during directed energy deposition (DED). They use an Optomec LENS MR-7 DED system (1 kW Nd:YAG, 1070 nm, 600 µm spot) equipped with four independent powder hoppers, each driven by an auger. Each hopper contained a single elemental powder (Cr, Fe, Mn, Ni) in gas-atomized 45–150 µm size, and composition control was achieved by varying auger RPMs (mass flow scales linearly with RPM). Powders are entrained in Ar carrier gas and melted by the laser; the build chamber is held at slight positive pressure with O2 <5 ppm. This in situ feeding enables fabrication of bulk samples with compositional gradients and high throughput (over 100 compositions in a week) with compositional control within ±5 at%. The workflow pairs DED synthesis with batch heat treatments, automated characterization (XRD, EDS, nanoindentation), and an active learning machine-learning loop (closed-loop design) to select new compositions. The primary powder-feeding approach described is multi-hopper auger-fed powder-feeders enabling dynamic mixing by flow-rate control.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'pegues2021exploringadditivemanufacturing pages 1-5'.

Jonathan W. Pegues, Michael A. Melia, Raymond Puckett, Shaun R. Whetten, Nicolas Argibay, and Andrew B. Kustas. Exploring additive manufacturing as a high-throughput screening tool for multiphase high entropy alloys. Additive Manufacturing, 37:101598, Jan 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598, doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598. This article has 108 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The paper describes using powder-based laser directed energy deposition (LB-DED) for high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery by in-situ alloying and compositional grading within a single specimen. The key method is fluidizing powder feedstock from multiple powder hoppers, each with a different powder composition, and using independently controlled flow rates to produce graded or stepped composition changes across a compact metallurgical sample. This approach enables rapid screening when coupled with high-throughput microstructural-mechanical characterization (e.g., mapping microstructure and hardness versus composition). Reported implementations and powder feeding systems include Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS™) and Optomec LENS platforms (MR-7, LENS-750), examples using single blended powder feedstock or two blended powders, and strategies combining a pre-alloyed build plate with additional elemental powder feeds. The cited studies demonstrate compositional grading of HEA and refractory systems (AlxCrCuFeNi, AlxCoCrFeNi, AlCrFeMoVx, TiZrNbTa, and elemental Mo/Nb/Ta/W) using blended or multi-hopper feeding to enable rapid exploration of phase and hardness across composition space.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'li2025highthroughputphasescreening pages 1-2'.

Jinlong Li, Xiaowei Zhang, Zhe An, Biqiang Li, Yizheng Wang, Yaoyuan Yang, Kexin Tong, and Yingze Zhu. High-throughput phase screening and laser-directed energy deposition of ti-ni-nb gradient alloys. Coatings, 15:401, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/coatings15040401, doi:10.3390/coatings15040401. This article has 0 citations.

The article describes a high-throughput combinatorial approach for discovering compositionally graded alloys using laser-directed energy deposition (DED). The primary experimental methods and systems include a multi-powder feeder capable of real-time continuously variable composition control, a multi-powder mixer, and a multi-powder nozzle to produce single-track Ti-Ni-Nb composition gradients. Prior work and this study emphasize the role of multi-powder mixers in stabilizing and homogenizing powder flow and note the use of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations to design feeders and nozzles and study powder flow characteristics. Analytical methods for screening and validating gradients include EDS to map local composition changes along the deposit and XRD to identify phase transitions across the gradient. The authors highlight the need for dynamic composition-adjusting approaches for multi-material DED and position their integrated feeder/mixer/nozzle system as enabling rapid alloy design by combining theoretical predictions with experimental phase screening. The described framework targets faster discovery and optimization of functionally graded materials for aerospace, biomedical, and structural applications.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 4-7'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This demonstration of high-throughput combinatorial alloy discovery uses Directed Energy Deposition (DED) together with an Alloy Development Feeder (ADF) — an automated 16-vial powder feeder — to produce a 16-member composition library by varying Nb, Mo, and Cr additions to an Inconel 625 base. The workflow couples composition selection guided by CALPHAD phase diagram modeling with automated DED powder feeding to realize compositional gradients across discrete samples. A ring-shaped sample geometry enables rotational indexing so beam geometries for XRD and SEM remain constant and many measurements can be automated. Automated characterization includes XRD, SEM-EDS/EBSD, instrumented micro- and nano-indentation, and per-sample phase mapping; all scans are largely fully automated and compatible with robotic SEM setups. Data-driven tools (Automatminer autoML) are integrated for property prediction (Vickers hardness) using composition and CALPHAD-provided features. The key powder feeding approach described is multi-vial automated feeding (ADF) to mix and deliver different elemental or pre-alloyed powders during DED builds, with AM powder characterization reported in supplementary materials.


---

## pqac-00000013

From text 'zhichao2024highthroughputanddatadriven pages 2-2'.

Lu Zhichao, Ma Dong, Liu Xiongjun, and Zhaoping Lu. High-throughput and data-driven machine learning techniques for discovering high-entropy alloys. Communications Materials, May 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s43246-024-00487-3, doi:10.1038/s43246-024-00487-3. This article has 57 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights directed energy deposition (DED) methods—specifically combinatorial laser additive manufacturing (LAM) and laser metal deposition (LMD)—as preferred high-throughput routes for creating compositional gradients and HEA libraries. LMD is emphasized for its real-time, variable powder feeding capability: feedstock nozzles deliver elemental powders into a laser-formed melt pool in an inert gas stream, and systems commonly employ two or more hoppers or multi-hopper feed systems to vary deposited compositions on the fly. Examples include double-powder-feeder arrangements (two hoppers loaded with different elemental blends) to generate graded compositions in height, and LENS/LMD variants used to build compositional and microstructural libraries. The approach enables rapid exploration (including refractory HEAs) by altering powder feeds during deposition; compositional gradients are produced by changing hopper feeds or feed rates, enabling mapping of composition–microstructure–property relationships and high-throughput mechanical screening (e.g., hardness mapping). The text documents use of blended elemental powders and multi-hopper feeders as practical powder-feeding strategies for mixing and dynamically varying multiple elemental powders during DED combinatorial experiments.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'zhichao2024highthroughputanddatadriven pages 1-2'.

Lu Zhichao, Ma Dong, Liu Xiongjun, and Zhaoping Lu. High-throughput and data-driven machine learning techniques for discovering high-entropy alloys. Communications Materials, May 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s43246-024-00487-3, doi:10.1038/s43246-024-00487-3. This article has 57 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes use of directed energy deposition (specifically LENS/LMD) for combinatorial alloy exploration by creating compositional gradients through layer-wise variation of powder feed. In the cited example a blended powder (nominal CrCuFeNi2) was used and the feeding rate of an additional element (Al) was incrementally increased per monolayer patch while laser power and travel speed were held constant. Two perpendicular remelting passes were applied after Al addition to enhance mixing and compositional homogeneity. Martin Seifrid, Robert Pollice, Andrés Aguilar-Granda, Zamyla Morgan Chan, Kazuhiro Hotta, Cher Tian Ser, Jenya Vestfrid, Tony C. Wu, and Alán Aspuru-Guzik. URL: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220, doi:10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220. This article has 320 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights that automated dispensing of powders is a major challenge for self-driving labs. Key difficulties include reliably dispensing very small masses (notably amounts below ~20 mg) and handling solids with widely varying physical properties; achieving both accuracy and precision demands substantial calibration and testing, which is often impractical in discovery workflows involving hundreds of different solids. Common work-arounds include avoiding direct powder dosing by using stock solutions and liquid-handling technologies; stock-solution preparation itself can be automated (e.g., aided by computer vision). Hardware and integration issues compound the problem: many instruments lack APIs or manufacturer support for full external control, so adapting equipment can require extensive custom coding or “hacking.” The text also notes related handling problems—heterogeneous mixtures and purification (e.g., SPE cartridges) can cause blockages and need human intervention—and mentions efforts to add robotic arms and online monitoring (computer vision) to imitate human loading actions and detect failures. The excerpt does not enumerate specific commercial powder dispensers or dosing devices by name.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000020

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 1-2'.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper reports a multi-method characterization of ten metal powders (316L, Zn, Sn, Al, Cu, Mn, Fe, Bronze, Ti, Mo) with focus on flowability for additive manufacturing feedstock. Measurements included particle size distribution (d10, d50, d90, span), morphology/shape (SEM), static and dynamic angle of repose (AOR, DAOR), tap density, and shear-based parameters: effective angle of internal friction (AIFE), cohesion and flow index obtained using three rotary shear devices (Ring Shear Tester RST-01.pc, Brookfield PFT Powder Flow Tester, and FT4 Powder Rheometer). Key findings: device-to-device values differed, so methodology and equipment affect results; by PSD and AOR, 50% of powders were free-flowing, whereas AIFE placed 20% in the lower area; flow-index classifications varied by instrument (100% free-flowing by RST-01.pc, 70% by PFT, 50% by FT4). The study highlights the importance of particle sphericity, size distribution and tap density for AM (packing, green density, sintering/porosity/shrinkage) and presents a comparison between AIFE and static AOR with a proposed conversion hypothesis. It emphasizes that flowability depends on material properties, stress state, equipment and handling.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 2-3'.

Jiri Zegzulka, Daniel Gelnar, Lucie Jezerska, Rostislav Prokes, and Jiri Rozbroj. Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. Scientific Reports, Dec 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes methods used to characterize metal powder flowability and cohesion for additive manufacturing feedstock and compares measurement devices. Flowability is presented as context-dependent (particle shape/size, humidity, stress state, equipment, handling). Common laboratory metrics include Carr’s index and Hausner ratio (from tap and bulk densities), static and dynamic angle of repose (AOR), and shear cell measurements with Jenike’s flow index (ffc = σ1/σc). Shear cells are widely used to rank powders, can measure bulk density versus normal stress, and yield the AIFE from shear tests. The paper compares three instruments (RST ring shear tester, Brookfield PFT, and FT4) and characterizes ten metal powders (316L, Zn, Sn, Al, Cu, Mn, Fe, Bronze, Ti, Mo). Particle size and shape were measured using laser diffraction (Cilas 1190) by the wet path with sonication to disperse particles. Prior inter-device comparisons cited show good agreement for free-flowing and moderately cohesive powders but large discrepancies for very cohesive materials; material type, consolidation level, and tester type significantly affect results. The authors note a lack of a single comprehensive flowability descriptor and emphasize instrument comparability for AM feedstock evaluation.

The excerpt cites a range of standardized test methods, instruments, models, and recent studies used to characterize metal powder flowability, cohesion, and dosing behavior for powder-bed additive manufacturing. Common quantitative measures and standards include tap density (ASTM B527), bulk density and angle of repose (ISO 4324), and powder rheology protocols (e.g., Anton Paar). Empirical instruments and tests named are Hall flowmeter and Arnold density meter, spreadability/recocating quality tests, and bespoke spreadability devices. Classical granular-flow models and hopper/orifice discharge methods (Beverloo, Johanson) and recent experimental flow-rate ranges for spherical metallic powders are cited for dosing and discharge prediction. Recent literature emphasizes the roles of particle size distribution, morphology, and agglomeration on flowability and LPBF manufacturability, and reports development of image-processing and experimental spreadability methods to quantify powder-layer quality. Overall, the referenced work shows a multi-method approach: density and flowmeter tests, rheometry, angle-of-repose, spreadability/ layer-quality experiments, and hopper-discharge modelling are combined to assess cohesion, flowability, and dosing accuracy of AM feedstocks.

From text 'zegzulka2020characterizationandflowability pages 15-17'.

URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3. This article has 144 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports multiple complementary methods used to characterize metal powder flow: static angle measures (AOR), combined effective-angle metrics (AIFE) obtained from FT4, RST and PFT devices, dynamic AOR using a rotating drum (varying frequency), bulk flow factor (ffc), particle size distribution Span, and SEM particle-shape imaging. Key findings: half the powders had Span < 1.5 (316 L, Zn, Cu, Fe, Bronze, Ti) indicating excellent flow. AOR classification split samples into Free-flowing (50%) and Fair-to-passable; Sn had the lowest AOR (30.2°) and Ti the highest (44.9°). Two AIFE/AOR groups were observed: one with AIFE ~29.9° (AOR ~33.74°) and a second with AIFE ~39.6° (AOR ~41.01°), with larger AIFE–AOR differences for Very Free-flowing/Free-flowing materials. Dynamic testing revealed three response patterns to drum speed (increasing angle, decreasing angle, minimal change) and showed lower dynamic AOR for powders with lower AIFE and higher ffc. ffc values depended on measurement shear-surface area (RST>PFT>FT4). SEM showed spherical/ smooth particles (316L, Zn, Sn, Al) correlated with lower AIFE; complex shapes correlated with higher AIFE. The authors conclude individual sample-specific assessment is required and note implications for 3D printing feedstock. The excerpt does not explicitly report dosing-accuracy measurements.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the details about the automated powder feeding system for graded thermoelectric materials? What are the specific powder feeder designs, feeding rates, accuracies, and results?", "text_name_focus_list": ["pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 1-2", "pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 2-5", "pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 5-7"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000028

From text 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 5-7'.

Hua-Yan Pu, Rong-Qing Xie, Yan Peng, Yang Yang, Shi-Yang He, Jun Luo, Yi Sun, Shao-Rong Xie, and Jun Luo. Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. Advances in Manufacturing, 7:278-287, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y, doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y. This article has 8 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The automated powder-feeding system uses three sequential feeders (glass containers, rotary table, charging unit, manipulator) controlled by a PLC (Mitsubishi FX3U-32MR/DS) with a PC front end; weight feedback from a balance is read over RS232 for closed-loop control. Feeding is implemented in two stages: a fast coarse stage (large protruded length L and high stirrer rotational speed V) and a precise stage (reduced L and V with PID position regulation). V was varied 20–230 r/min (step 35 r/min); maximum feeding occurred at L = 8 mm, V = 230 r/min and minimum at L = 1 mm, V = 20 r/min. Overall feeding rates ranged from ~0.19 mg/s to 15.27 mg/s. Example Bi powder runs showed the fast+precise sequence completed in under 3 minutes (protrusion rose to 7.2 mm and V to 160 r/min, then reduced to 6 mm and 130 r/min). PID gains used were KP = 0.6, KI = 0.4, KD = 1.5. Reported per-component feeding errors were <0.1 mg across samples, demonstrating good accuracy and repeatability, and the system reduced sample preparation time by ~50% versus manual dosing.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 7-9'.

Hua-Yan Pu, Rong-Qing Xie, Yan Peng, Yang Yang, Shi-Yang He, Jun Luo, Yi Sun, Shao-Rong Xie, and Jun Luo. Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. Advances in Manufacturing, 7:278-287, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y, doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y. This article has 8 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes an automated powder feeding system for producing compositional-gradient (graded) thermoelectric samples. The system operates with gravimetric feedback and was designed for continuous, fast, reliable, and accurate dosing of components to produce gradient BixSb2-xTe3 samples. Feeding rate and accuracy are controlled by adjusting the protruded length and the rotational speed of a feeder stirrer. Dosing accuracy was high: "the feeding errors of all the components were less than 0.1 mg" (Table 3 provides per-layer errors for Bi, Sb, Te). The automated workflow also reduced preparation time compared with manual operation ("the time-cost savings was approximately 50% by the system"). Process validation included hot-pressing, annealing (which removed Sb impurities), SEM-EDXS mapping (a 7.5 mm slab region divided into 14 areas), and compositional analyses showing a clear gradient ("the content of the element Bi increased from the bottom to the top side"). The system produced reproducible, single-phase graded materials after sintering, and authors note its potential as a low-cost solution for materials and pharmaceutical development.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'pu2019acceleratingsamplepreparation pages 1-2'.

Hua-Yan Pu, Rong-Qing Xie, Yan Peng, Yang Yang, Shi-Yang He, Jun Luo, Yi Sun, Shao-Rong Xie, and Jun Luo. Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. Advances in Manufacturing, 7:278-287, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y, doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y. This article has 8 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper describes an automated, gravimetric powder feeding system built for accelerating sample preparation of functionally graded thermoelectric materials. The implemented system uses three dosing feeders and operates on gravimetric principles; feeding rate and accuracy are controlled by adjusting the protruded length L (mm) and the rotational speed V (r/min) of a feeder stirrer. The authors emphasize that gravimetric feeding is better suited for accurate low (micro) dosing and that conventional manual weighing is slow and error-prone (notably for doses below ~200 mg). The system was demonstrated by preparing graded BixSb2-xTe3 (x = 0.3–0.8) samples; after hot-pressing, the material crystallized in the rhombohedral phase and showed a good gradient composition distribution, validating the feeding approach. The excerpt contains design concept and control strategy and the successful materials outcome, but does not provide explicit numeric feed-rate ranges or quantitative accuracy metrics for the developed feeders (only background figures for other volumetric methods are mentioned elsewhere in the text).


---

Hua-Yan Pu, Rong-Qing Xie, Yan Peng, Yang Yang, Shi-Yang He, Jun Luo, Yi Sun, Shao-Rong Xie, and Jun Luo. Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. Advances in Manufacturing, 7:278-287, Aug 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y, doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y. This article has 8 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper describes an automated powder feeding system for producing graded thermoelectric (FGM) samples composed of Bi, Sb, and Te powders. The system has three modules: a feeding module with horizontal and vertical motorized stages, a rotating unit, and three dosing feeders (Mettler Toledo QH012-ZNMW 11150115). Each dosing feeder uses a stirrer with a thin, long groove: when positioned under the shaft the stirrer rotates and slides down so powder outflows from a glass container through the groove into a small reservoir on a balance. Closed-loop control uses a micro balance (Mettler Toledo XSE105DU) with 0.01 mg readability and 0–41 g range to control dosing. A transmission module with a charging unit and a 6-DoF UR3 manipulator moves reservoirs to/from the balance. Feeding rate and accuracy are controlled by coordinating stirrer rotational speed V (r/min) and protruded length L (mm); a larger L exposes a larger groove (more powder) while higher V improves flowability. The paper notes L has a stronger effect on rate, while V responds faster for fine tuning. The system implements staged (fast and precise) feeding based on the error between target and measured mass. Powder properties and layer recipes for six-layer FGMs are given (Bi/Sb/Te purities, mesh sizes, bulk densities and per-layer masses).

The provided excerpt describes several powder-feeder types and lists commercial micro-feeders with their nominal mass flow ranges and applicable powder properties, but it does not describe an automated feeding system specifically for graded thermoelectric materials. Rotary valves: rotor+housing design where the inlet fills and a motor-driven rotor transfers powder under gravity; they are simple to control by rotor speed but are sensitive to particle size and cohesion and "are typically only used in feeding easy-flowing materials." Venturi feeders: use the venturi effect/air flow to convey powder, with feed rate controlled by air flow; advantages include "simplicity" and no moving parts, while disadvantages include limited feed-rate range, wear, and particle degradation. Table 2-1 lists commercial micro-feeders and nominal mass flow rates (g/h) for models: K-Tron MT-12 & MT-20 ("32 - 3540"), MG2 Microdose ("0.36 - 0.54"), 3P Innovation vibratory ("3.6 - 1440"), LCI MD-120 (">32"), DEC µPTS ("3.6-450"), Vibra-flow feeder ("<3.6"). Powder properties handled (when listed) include particle size ranges, bulk densities, and cohesion notes. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. The excerpt describes the powder feeding hardware and volumetric characterization protocol used in the study. A K-Tron MT12 twin-screw co-rotating loss-in-weight (LIW) feeder was used (max screw speed 60 rpm). Concave coarse screws: 12 mm diameter, 5.75 mm pitch; hopper volume 1 L with an agitator; no automatic refill and no manual refills during experiments. Feed data (screw speed, net weight, etc.) were logged every 1 s. A catch scale beneath the outlet recorded raw powder feed rate every second (used instead of the feeder’s processed feed rate). Volumetric tests fed each powder at three screw speeds: 10%, 50%, 90% of max (6, 30, 54 rpm). The primary metric is feed factor (g per revolution), computed from catch-scale feed rate and screw speed; from feed factor profiles the authors extract FFmax (maximum feeding capacity) and FFdecay (hopper fill % where feed factor drops to 90% of FFmax). Data disturbances (beaker replacement) were removed and profiles smoothed with a 20 s moving average. These descriptions provide feeder geometry, operating speeds, data-logging cadence, and defined performance metrics (FFmax, FFdecay) relevant for assessing feeder design and feeding behavior.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the key findings of the Li et al. 2018 paper on combinatorial metallurgical synthesis of high-entropy alloys? What methods are described for rapid alloy prototyping and laser additive manufacturing for HEA exploration?", "text_name_focus_list": ["li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 1-2", "li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 2-4", "li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 4-6", "li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 6-7"]}
