messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000034

From text 'li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 1-2'.

Zhiming Li, Alfred Ludwig, Alan Savan, Hauke Springer, and Dierk Raabe. Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. Journal of Materials Research, 33:3156-3169, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1557/jmr.2018.214, doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214. This article has 160 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Li et al. (2018) review combinatorial, high-throughput experimental routes to accelerate exploration of the vast compositional space of high-entropy alloys (HEAs). They present and compare four principal methods: rapid alloy prototyping (RAP), diffusion-multiples, laser additive manufacturing (LAM), and combinatorial co-deposition of multinary thin-film materials libraries. RAP is described as an integrated bulk workflow coupling casting, hot rolling, homogenization, cold rolling, and recrystallization annealing with downstream microstructural and property characterization; RAP can cast multiple tuned alloys in a single run but faces as-cast compositional inhomogeneity and dendritic (Scheil) segregation that necessitate thermomechanical processing (e.g., hot rolling of 10 mm blocks) to remove dendrites and porosity. LAM (the third approach) enables synthesis of compositionally graded, additively manufactured materials suitable for probing many variants rapidly. The authors note the complementarity of bulk methods (which allow subsequent processing and microstructure adaptation) and thin-film methods (which can cover wider composition ranges and pair with high-throughput characterization). They also emphasize coupling these experimental methods with theory to focus screenings on target phase/metastability and properties.


---

## pqac-00000035

From text 'li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 4-6'.

Zhiming Li, Alfred Ludwig, Alan Savan, Hauke Springer, and Dierk Raabe. Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. Journal of Materials Research, 33:3156-3169, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1557/jmr.2018.214, doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214. This article has 160 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports experimental and methodological findings relevant to combinatorial HEA development. For a Fe–Mn–Co–Cr–C iHEA, compositional inhomogeneity (Mn-depleted, Fe-enriched regions) lowers local stacking fault energy and promotes HCP island formation, causing preferred deformation-driven FCC→HCP transformation, early stress–strain localization, and reduced ductility/strain-hardening in as-cast coarse-grained samples; homogenization produces a more uniform, dual-phase (FCC+HCP) microstructure with equiaxed FCC grains and improved properties. RAP (rapid alloy prototyping) can produce bulk samples enabling conventional characterizations (XRD, EBSD, EDS, tensile testing), but a single RAP run probes only a limited number of compositions (generally ~5). The authors emphasize theoretical guidance—parameter-free ab initio and thermodynamic calculations—to predict stacking fault energies/free-energy differences and guide combinatorial design. Diffusion-multiple assemblies are described for high-throughput exploration of multicomponent systems: building assemblies of pure/alloy blocks, careful surface polishing/cleaning, vacuum welding (e.g., electron beam welding), and hot isostatic pressing (e.g., ~1200 °C) to induce interdiffusion and composition gradients. For characterization, EBSD has limitations for unknown or closely related phases; site-specific FIB sample prep combined with TEM is recommended to resolve crystal structures. The provided excerpt does not describe laser additive manufacturing methods.


---

## pqac-00000036

From text 'li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 6-7'.

Zhiming Li, Alfred Ludwig, Alan Savan, Hauke Springer, and Dierk Raabe. Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. Journal of Materials Research, 33:3156-3169, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1557/jmr.2018.214, doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214. This article has 160 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The provided excerpt highlights that Li et al. (2018) identify the diffusion-multiple technique as a powerful combinatorial approach for exploring multicomponent high-entropy alloys (HEAs). The paper emphasizes rapid, microscale mapping of composition–structure–property relationships using localized characterization methods: EMPA for composition, EBSD for crystal structure identification, FIB-TEM for site-specific structural analysis, instrumented nanoindentation and dynamic AFM for hardness and elastic-modulus mapping, time-domain thermoreflectance for thermal conductivity, and FIB-enabled micro/nanopillars for compressive-strength/ductility estimation. A specific application cited (Wilson et al.) used a Co–Cr–Fe–Mn–Ni diffusion multiple to identify a disordered quinary FCC region; nanoindentation versus atomic-mismatch data showed that maximum hardness did not align with maximum atomic mismatch, challenging the notion that severe lattice distortion is the dominant strengthening mechanism in that HEA. The authors note diffusion multiples can, in principle, produce extensive composition libraries at relatively low cost, though some composition regions may be too small to characterize. For rapid alloy prototyping and AM exploration, the paper also references laser additive methods: both laser metal deposition (LMD) and selective laser melting (SLM) have been used to produce bulk HEAs, with Joseph et al. demonstrating LMD fabrication of AlxCoCrFeNi alloys from elemental powder blends.


---

## pqac-00000037

From text 'li2018combinatorialmetallurgicalsynthesis pages 2-4'.

Zhiming Li, Alfred Ludwig, Alan Savan, Hauke Springer, and Dierk Raabe. Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. Journal of Materials Research, 33:3156-3169, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1557/jmr.2018.214, doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214. This article has 160 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the RAP (rapid alloy prototyping / combinatorial metallurgical) approach for bulk HEA screening, emphasizing a continuous high-throughput processing chain: multiple casting, hot-rolling, high-temperature homogenization and cutting, cold-rolling, annealing/cutting, and characterization. Practical parameters and considerations are given: hot-rolling reduction and cold-rolling thickness reduction are typically large (cold-rolling >50%) to achieve fully deformed microstructures; homogenization (e.g., 1200 °C for several hours under Ar followed by water quench) is critical because low substitutional diffusion rates in HEAs require sufficient time for chemical homogenization, and thicker sheets need longer homogenization. The text notes persistent issues such as Mn-enriched inclusions that can be hard to remove, and that identical bulk compositions processed differently can show markedly different compositional homogeneity, microstructures, and mechanical behavior (e.g., loss of work-hardening in compositionally inhomogeneous interstitial HEAs due to localized shear). For accelerated throughput, machining efficiency is increased by clamping multiple alloy segments for simultaneous processing. The excerpt includes an example interstitial HEA (Fe49.5Mn30Co10Cr10C0.5) showing coarse as-cast FCC matrices with HCP islands correlated with local Mn depletion. The excerpt does not include details on laser additive manufacturing methods for HEA exploration.

## pqac-00000038

From text 'pegues2021exploringadditivemanufacturing pages 1-5'.

Jonathan W. Pegues, Michael A. Melia, Raymond Puckett, Shaun R. Whetten, Nicolas Argibay, and Andrew B. Kustas. Exploring additive manufacturing as a high-throughput screening tool for multiphase high entropy alloys. Additive Manufacturing, 37:101598, Jan 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598, doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598. This article has 108 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports that Li et al. (2018) studied the AlxCoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy using an Optomec MR-7 LENS system, employing a pre-alloyed CoCrFeNi build plate with added Al powder to vary composition. Their key reported finding was phase stability behavior: composition-driven transitions among FCC, BCC and B2-type phases were analyzed in terms of composition and materials parameters such as valence electron concentration, atomic size, and electronegativity differences. The excerpt also summarizes the broader rapid-prototyping methods used in similar AM-HTMD studies: laser-based directed energy deposition (LB-DED/LENS) enables in situ alloying and creation of graded/stepped compositional libraries by fluidizing powder feedstock from multiple hoppers with independently controlled flow rates. This approach permits combinatorial synthesis of many compositions within a single metallurgical sample and, when coupled with high-throughput microstructural–mechanical characterization (e.g., rapid mapping of microstructure and hardness vs. position/composition), enables fast screening of HEA phase and property space.

What powder feeding performance metrics, displacement-feed factor, and accuracy results are reported?", "text_name_focus_list": ["fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 1-2", "fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3", "fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 4-5", "fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 5-7"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000039

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a micro-feeding system that combines volumetric piston displacement with external feed-rate monitoring via a high-precision balance to feed powders from below 1 g/h up to ~100 g/h. The system uses a cartridge with a movable piston (cartridge dimensions used: r = 11.5 mm, h = 100 mm), a scraper, a top plate with two openings, a chute, and an anti-static kit; piston displacement is controlled by a precision syringe pump and automated data recording was implemented. The authors define an “effective displacement density” (Δm/ΔV) and a normalized “relative effective displacement density” (ρ*ED = (Δm)/(ΔV ρT)) as a way to relate volumetric displacement to mass at the outlet. The manuscript notes the need for pre-conditioning to minimize axial bulk-density variation and wall-friction-induced in-process densification; it tests feeding performance for multiple materials (two lactose grades, dicalcium phosphate, croscarmellose sodium, silicon dioxide, a model API, and an SDI). The excerpt contains system design, tested materials, operating range, and a metric definition, but does not include numeric displacement-feed factors or quantitative accuracy/error results (those likely appear elsewhere in the paper).


---

## pqac-00000040

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 4-5'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Excerpted methods and results describe the micro-feeder experimental setup and selected performance metrics. The feeder used a scraper operating at 10 rpm (one rotation every 6 s) with material discharge recorded at 1 s intervals; experimental setup and parameters were kept constant except for displacement. All runs were pre-conditioned (finger tapping and pre-compaction to tapped density). Particle size distributions were measured with a HELOS/KR laser diffraction system and bulk/tapped densities with a Pharmatest PT-TD200 following USP. Reported feeding performance: achievable feed rates span roughly 1.5–15 g/h with reproducible feed curves (reproducibility shown for lowest and highest feed rates) and overall feed-rate deviations of about 10–20% for a constant piston speed. The study uses a "relative effective displacement density, ρ*ED," and states that feed rate = ρ*ED × displaced volume/time (i.e., displacement-feed relation). Observations include density changes during feeding (ρ*ED evolving from <1 to >1), continuous densification for cohesive fine powders, and shorter feed durations for very low-density silica. Compressibility (ΔL% at 15 kPa) and elastic recovery after de-pressurization were also quantified.


---

## pqac-00000041

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 12-13'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports key operational details of the Fathollahi et al. 2020 micro-feeder for low-dose powder feeding. The authors define a “displacement-feed factor” and report that piston (SDI) speeds corresponding to feed rates of 1, 5, 10, and 15 g/h are 0.1, 0.46, 0.93, and 1.39 mm/min, respectively. They state that the displacement–feed dependence can be used to control piston speed to achieve a constant and low feed rate over the tested range (1–20 g/h). Segregation is noted as not being a significant issue for this process. A prominent practical advantage is the small amount of material needed for testing (tens of mL versus 1–2 kg for larger feeders), which is important for potent APIs and early development. The authors indicate planned future work to implement closed-loop control for integration into GMP continuous manufacturing and to automate pre-conditioning (replacing manual finger tapping with automated periodic hammering of the cartridge). The excerpt does not include numerical accuracy metrics (e.g., percent error, bias, or precision/CV) or detailed performance statistics, so those specific accuracy results must be taken from other sections of the paper.

## pqac-00000042

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 5-7'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes feeding experiments with a novel micro-feeder for low-dose powder delivery and introduces the relative effective displacement density (ρ*ED) as the link between displaced volume and mass: "The feed rate is equal to ρ*ED times the displaced volume per time, which is a constant." The device was tested across multiple powders and low feed rates (examples: silicon dioxide at 1.5 and 2.5 g/h; CapsuLac60 at 5 g/h; GranuLac200 at 10 g/h; di-calcium phosphate at 10–15 g/h). The micro-feeder could handle very low-density, cohesive powders (SiO2, bulk 0.04 g/cm3) "without significant fluctuations." Powder properties (particle size, bulk/tapped density, compressibility, elastic recovery) strongly affected feeding: larger particles and lower compressibility showed initial higher feed rates due to densification, while fines/compressible powders densified less. Non-uniform expansion of the powder bed after pre-conditioning caused initially elevated feed rates followed by lower density/feed-rate phases. The manuscript states that ρ*ED and relative standard deviation (RSD) for all materials are summarized in Table V, implying reported accuracy and variability metrics (RSD, ρ*ED) are provided there though numeric RSD/accuracy values are not present in this excerpt.

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 1-2'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Pages 1–2 describe a volumetric micro-feeder designed for continuous low-dose powder feeding and introduce the displacement-feed factor concept. The system combines a volumetric piston-type feeder with an outlet weighing balance to measure output rates; piston motion is driven by a linear actuator and controlled (piston speed via a syringe pump). The study tested a range of materials (two α-lactose grades, dicalcium phosphate, croscarmellose sodium, silicon dioxide, a spray-dried intermediate, and an API) to vary particle size and flow properties. Authors present feeding results as a “displacement-feed factor” curve per material, using known powder mass and volume to characterize density variation during processing; this profile is intended for calibration and predicting performance at any feed rate. They report qualitative relations between powder properties and behavior: finer, more compressible powders show densification, while coarser, less compressible powders show densification plus powder bed expansion (dilation and elastic recovery). The excerpt outlines hardware and the analytical approach but does not include numeric feed-rate ranges, quantitative displacement-feed factor data, or specific accuracy/performance metrics, which are likely reported later in the paper.

This article has 23 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the micro-feeder used for low-dose API feeding: a vertically oriented stainless-steel cartridge (41.5 cm³) with a Teflon piston driven by a syringe pump; a scraper on a horizontal plate (10 rpm) moves powder from the cartridge through a hole into a glass funnel and a vertical stainless-steel pipe to the extruder. The piston displacement (operated between 0.1 and 0.5 mm/min in this study) controls the displaced powder; feed rate and displacement speed correspond and the correct displacement speed for a target feed rate is determined empirically via separate test runs. During calibration runs powder was collected on a high-precision balance (XPE 204) with mass data recorded at 1 s intervals. The API is manually filled and pre-conditioned by tapping to avoid densification. Representative feed rates for fenofibrate in the experiments were 1, 2 and 5 g/h. The excerpt does not report numeric displacement-to-mass conversion factors (displacement-feed factor) or detailed accuracy/error statistics; it refers to Fathollahi (2020) and later sections/figures (e.g., Figure 3) for additional feeding performance results.

The excerpt describes a micro-feeder prototype that uses active volumetric displacement to deliver low-dose powders in a hot-melt extrusion (HME) line. It successfully fed API at low rates (1–5 g/h). Tests showed the device can achieve constant, reproducible feed rates after an initial start-up phase, during which powder densification in the cartridge occurs. The authors demonstrated split feeding (micro-feeder for API plus a conventional loss-in-weight (LIW) feeder for polymer) and note cartridges can be sized for full-shift production. The paper also proposes adding a small accurate balance to the micro-feeder (converting it to a loss-in-weight setup) to provide direct mass measurement and reduce operator influence. function.arguments: {"question": "What are the details of the HT-READ (high-throughput rapid experimental alloy development) system by Vecchio et al. 2021? What is the Alloy Development Feeder, and what automated characterization methods are described?", "text_name_focus_list": ["vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 1-4", "vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 4-7", "vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 7-11"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000047

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 7-11'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The HT-READ pipeline integrates directed-energy-deposition (DED) additive manufacturing, computational screening (CALPHAD), machine learning, and automated/semiautomated characterization using a 16-sample circular "ring" library (32 mm OD) that enables rapid fabrication and minimal realignment (samples printed at 22.5° apart). The hardware includes a Formalloy DED system equipped with a Formalloy Alloy Development Feeder (ADF) powder hopper containing 16 vials that rotate under computer control so one vial feeds the DED head at a time, allowing up to 16 distinct compositions per build. Example processing conditions: 450 nm laser (150 W available), builds on IN625 substrate at 130–150 W and 750 mm/min feed, followed by solutionizing at 1100°C for 1 h. Feedstock powders: IN625, Nb, Mo, Cr with 200 ppm alumina flow aid; powders were premixed on a rolling mill and loaded into vials (authors note in-situ powder mixing alternatives and potential automation of weighing/mixing). Automated or semi-automated characterization described includes XRD (custom rotating holder for 16-sample ring), SEM/EDS, EBSD, nanoindentation/Vickers hardness, and electron microscopy; CALPHAD and ML are used for phase and hardness prediction. Sample prep used waterjet cutting, mounting, and sequential polishing (SiC to 4000 grit, 3 µm/1 µm diamond, 0.04 µm colloidal silica); authors note commercial automated polishing and autonomous SEM transfer exist to further automate the loop.


---

## pqac-00000048

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 33-50'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The HT-READ system described includes an Alloy Development Feeder (ADF, Formalloy) that holds up to 16 vials, where "Each of the 16 vials can contain a different alloy composition." The ADF features automated rotation that is "controlled through the additive build program," enabling systematic feeding of multiple compositions during additive processing. For automated characterization, the authors demonstrate automated sample rotation and data collection in XRD and SEM: XRD (Panalytical X’Pert Pro) samples are rotated about their center axis ("Sample rotates every 10 seconds") with a stated "Data collection time per sample is approximately 10 minutes." In the SEM, samples are similarly rotated ("Automated rotation of the sample about its center axis in the SEM, side view."), with rotation used so that "beam geometry [stays] constant when the sample is rotated during automated data collection." Automated SEM-based chemical and microstructure mapping ("Automated SEM-EDS/EBSD data collection") is reported with "Data collection time per sample is approximately 1 minute." Appendix B provides CALPHAD phase-evolution diagrams for the sample set (e.g., "CALPHAD data for sample 1: In625" and many alloy variants), indicating integration of computational phase predictions with the high-throughput experimental workflow.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 4-7'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

Vecchio et al. demonstrate an integrated HT-READ workflow built around a 16-sample ring-shaped library derived from Inconel 625. Libraries were fabricated by Directed Energy Deposition (DED) using an Alloy Development Feeder (ADF) — described as "an automated 16-vial powder feeder" — to deposit 16 compositional variants (base Inconel 625 plus graded Nb, Mo, and Cr additions). The ring geometry maintains beam geometries during rotation, enabling automated, repeated XRD and SEM scans as the sample rotates. Characterization is fully automated: 16-sample XRD, 16-sample SEM-EDS/EBSD, fiducial-pattern micro-indentation, and nanoindentation are collected without manual intervention. Automated SEM-EDS/EBSD provides phase location, size, morphology, and—via fiducial markers—phase-specific mechanical properties. The workflow couples CALPHAD phase predictions with machine learning: Automatminer (autoML) used compositional inputs and CALPHAD info to predict Vickers hardness; a Gradient Boosting Regressor was selected and correctly identified three of the five hardest experimental compositions. The system supports robotic SEM integration and emphasizes interchangeable AI agents for analysis (e.g., EBSD/XRD/EDS fusion) to accelerate alloy exploration.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'vecchio2021highthroughputrapidexperimental pages 1-4'.

Kenneth S. Vecchio, Olivia F. Dippo, Kevin R. Kaufmann, and Xiao Liu. High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (ht-read). Acta Materialia, 221:117352, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352. This article has 95 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the HT-READ framework as an integrated, closed-loop high-throughput alloy development methodology that unifies computational screening, automated fabrication of sample libraries, automated characterization, and AI-driven analysis. Computational screening uses CALPHAD and machine-learning models to recommend sample compositions, and an artificial intelligence agent is used to correlate composition and properties. Samples are fabricated as a discrete library (a 32 mm metallurgical mount containing a ring of 16 individual samples) using 3D printing, with each library assigned unique identifiers for persistent data/sample tracking. A major bottleneck identified is comprehensive microstructural characterization; the authors emphasize combined XRD, SEM imaging, EDS, and EBSD as the primary characterization suite. To enable automation, they design sample geometry around a rotational center of symmetry, noting "significant advantages of only employing sample rotations to position from one sample to the next, rather than positioning by X-Y-Z translations." The excerpt does not mention or define an "Alloy Development Feeder" device or component within HT-READ.

Brenden Pelkie, Sterling Baird, Eunice Aissi, Kenzo Aspuru-Takata, Yang Cao, Jin Hyun Chang, Kshitij Gambhir, Wm Salt Hale, Lucy Hao, Chance Hattrick, Jason Hein, Danli Luo, Owen Melville, Monique Ngan, Louie Lucas Bisgaard Nyeland, Nadya Peek, Maria Politi, Ethan Elliot Rajkumar, Alexander Siemenn, Blair Subbaraman, Sonya Vasquez, Jeffrey Watchorn, Wenyu Zhang, Rógvi Ziskason, Lilo Pozzo, Tonio Buonassisi, and Tejs Vegge. This article has 9 citations.

The Pelkie et al. (2025) excerpt describes multiple open-hardware, low-cost automation devices and modular 3D-printed lab equipment intended for self-driving labs. The LEDbyXample photoreactor is an inexpensive, modular, 3D-printed-frame system with swappable ~1W LED modules on heat sinks, a magnet-mounted fan for stirring, optional active cooling, and control via custom PCBs plus a Raspberry Pi Pico running simple Python; full build documentation and parts lists are provided. A standalone powder-dispensing module is referenced (Figure 1) but no operational details are given in the excerpt. The rolling ball viscometer measures Newtonian viscosity using the rolling-ball principle and Stoke’s law, records ball motion with a high-speed camera, and supports automated sample loading/cleaning via peristaltic pumps. The color-mixing bot demonstrates end-to-end automation with peristaltic pumps, RGB sensor, pH probe, and a multi-objective Bayesian optimizer. The DiSCO platform integrates modular synthesis (Archerfish combinatorial printing for 10-dimensional drop-cast synthesis), automated optical/contact characterization, machine-learning control, and a linear rail for sample transfer. The excerpt does not contain details from Lo 2024 (frugal twin) or Doloi 2025 regarding powder handling or open-source hardware.

The platform emphasizes extensibility (tool-changing capability), documented open-hardware tools for liquid handling, imaging, and sonication, a Python library for high-level experiment programming, and strong community support and documentation. A highlighted 3D-printed device, the Digital Pipette, is presented as a low-cost (< $100 USD) liquid handling solution with replaceable fluid-contacting parts, luer-lock syringe integration, a self-contained linear servo actuator, and 3D-printed frame components. The project added a 3D-printed mounting attachment and MQTT communication so the pipette can mount on motion platforms (science-jubilee or robotic arms) and interoperate with other devices; this integration has been reproduced by multiple groups. The platform bridges the capability/cost gap between hacked 3D printers and commercial automation and supports integration of commercial instruments (e.g., Opentrons OT2, BluRev RDE) for automated workflows. The excerpt does not describe powder-dispensing modules, nor does it mention Lo 2024 "frugal twin" or Doloi 2025 papers on powder handling.

Contributors often motivate projects by much lower bills of materials than commercial systems, but Pelkie warns that these savings can be offset by “hundreds to thousands of hours” of development time. Replication and wide adoption of open hardware can realize cost benefits. The paper stresses that mere CAD files/parts lists are insufficient: high-quality, maintained documentation is mandatory (“For hardware, documentation is the source…it transforms a box of screws and a spool of 3D printer filament into a productive component”). It also highlights safe practice limits (avoid ad-hoc builds for hazardous equipment) and suggests hybrid approaches that integrate commercial modules (examples: integrating commercial pipettor and off-the-shelf hyperspectral imaging). Education (practical electrical/mechanical skills) and community forums/documentation incentives are needed to scale democratized SDLs. The custom design uses a stepper motor driving a precision auger plus an integrated balance for closed-loop feedback control; powder is dispensed into a disposable syringe body and mixed with liquid via a peristaltic pump. It highlights that inexpensive FDM printers (initial investment typically under $350) plus off-the-shelf electronics (Arduino, Raspberry Pi, Beagleboard) enable custom labware and even repurposing of printer motion systems into liquid-handling and sample-preparation robotic platforms. Key limitations include limited resolution (typical layer heights 100–300 µm, nozzle diameters ≥0.4 mm), material durability issues (PLA/ABS creep and fatigue), and the need for post-processing or finishing (sanding, chemical smoothing such as acetone for ABS, or sealing with biocompatible epoxy/UV resin). Strategies to improve longevity and functionality include design features (fillets, wear-resistant bushings), embedding conductive fillers for sensors/electronics, and modular, parametric designs to improve reproducibility and supply-chain flexibility. The text also contrasts low-cost DIY solutions with commercial liquid handlers (Opentrons OT-2 ≈ $10,000; higher-end systems $50–60k). messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| # | Topic | Citation | Summary | Relevance to low-cost open-hardware multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | (a) Multi-material AM powder deposition | Schneck et al. 2021, *Progress in Additive Manufacturing*, 6:881-894, doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2 | This review investigates multi-material metal manufacturing using powder bed fusion, detailing hardware architectures like nozzle-based, masked, and holohedral deposition concepts (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002). Nozzle-based systems enable localized dispensing but face critical challenges regarding powder flowability and interparticular forces. The study notes most hardware remains at a laboratory readiness level with deposition rates up to 64 mg/s. | Defines the mechanical state-of-the-art and hardware constraints for nozzle-based powder deposition, establishing baseline performance metrics for new open-hardware dosers. |
| 2 | (a) Multi-material AM powder deposition | Mehrpouya et al. 2022, *Rapid Prototyping Journal*, 28:1-19, doi:10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014 | This paper provides a comprehensive overview of multi-material powder bed fusion techniques, focusing on practical powder deposition implementations (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006). It highlights dual-hopper systems for on-demand mixing and patterning drums for selective voxel deposition. The authors underscore that hardware for discrete, cross-contamination-free multi-powder dosing is still highly experimental. | Highlights existing multi-hopper and mixing mechanisms, providing direct mechanical inspiration for designing a contamination-free blending doser. |
| 3 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Li et al. 2018, *J. Materials Research*, 33:3156-3169, doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214 | This work compares combinatorial synthesis methods, including rapid alloy prototyping and laser additive manufacturing, for exploring high-entropy alloys (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000036). Laser metal deposition from elemental powder blends is identified as highly effective for generating bulk compositionally graded materials. The authors emphasize coupling automated physical synthesis with theoretical thermodynamic predictions. | Provides the fundamental metallurgical justification for building automated multi-powder additive systems to accelerate high-entropy alloy discovery. |
| 4 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Pegues et al. 2021, *Additive Manufacturing*, 37:101598, doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598 | The authors utilize laser-based directed energy deposition to rapidly screen multi-phase HEAs via in-situ alloying (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000038). By fluidizing feedstocks from multiple hoppers with independently controlled flow rates, they fabricated compositionally graded samples. This rapid synthesis was coupled with high-throughput mechanical characterization to map phase stability. | Validates the use of independent flow-rate control across multiple hoppers for combinatorial discovery, a core capability required for the targeted open-hardware doser. |
| 5 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Vecchio et al. 2021, *Acta Materialia*, 221:117352, doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352 | This paper presents HT-READ, a closed-loop methodology uniting computational screening, DED fabrication, and machine learning (pqac-00000047, pqac-00000049). An automated 16-vial powder feeder was used to deposit compositional variants of Inconel 625 into a ring library geometry. This geometry enabled fully automated XRD and SEM-EDS scanning to correlate microstructure with hardness. | Showcases a highly successful multi-vial automated powder feeding strategy; replicating this via low-cost hardware would democratize state-of-the-art combinatorial workflows. |
| 6 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Nelaturu et al. 2024, *Materials Science and Engineering: A*, 891:145945, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945 | This study details a high-throughput DED workflow utilizing an active machine-learning loop for discovering multi-principal element alloys (pqac-00000009). The authors achieved dynamic, in-situ alloying using four independent auger-driven powder hoppers, maintaining compositional accuracy within ±5 at%. This allowed the synthesis and characterization of over 100 compositions in a single week. | Proves that auger-driven feeders can deliver the necessary precision (±5 at%) for ML-driven alloy design, validating auger-based metering for the custom doser. |
| 7 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Li et al. 2025, *Coatings*, 15:401, doi:10.3390/coatings15040401 | The researchers developed an integrated DED setup featuring a multi-powder feeder, a multi-powder mixer, and a custom nozzle for real-time composition control (pqac-00000011). They successfully fabricated Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys with verified phase transitions along a single deposition track. A key finding is the critical role of the in-line powder mixer in stabilizing and homogenizing flow before laser melting. | Demonstrates that precision dosing alone is insufficient; an in-line powder mixing module is essential for homogeneous alloy discovery. |
| 8 | (b) Combinatorial/graded alloy discovery | Liu et al. 2024, *Materials Genome Engineering Advances*, 2(3), doi:10.1002/mgea.55 | This comprehensive review contrasts traditional trial-and-error alloy design with high-throughput DED and LPBF preparation methods (pqac-00000008). It evaluates techniques for generating compositional gradients, including multi-hopper systems for Z-axis variations and gradient powder spreading for horizontal variations. The authors note that precise real-time composition control remains a significant bottleneck in many current AM setups. | Identifies real-time composition control as a primary industry bottleneck, emphasizing the exact capability gap the open-hardware doser aims to bridge. |
| 9 | (c) Self-driving labs / autonomous experimentation | Tom et al. 2024, *Chemical Reviews*, 124:9633-9732, doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.4c00055 | Covering the state-of-the-art in self-driving laboratories, this review highlights the extreme difficulty and cost of solid powder dispensing compared to liquid handling (pqac-00000016). Challenges include handling cohesive media and integrating real-time mass measurements in autonomous loops. The authors document how high commercial costs are driving the development of open-source and 3D-printed hardware solutions (pqac-00000057). | Confirms that powder handling is a major barrier in autonomous labs and strongly supports the use of open-source, 3D-printed hardware to overcome it. |
| 10 | (c) Self-driving labs / autonomous experimentation | Lo et al. 2024, *Digital Discovery*, 3:842-868, doi:10.1039/d3dd00223c | This paper introduces the frugal twin concept, advocating for low-cost, accessible surrogates of expensive autonomous experiment setups (pqac-00000058). The authors detail open-hardware projects that leverage 3D printing and microcontrollers to build modular execution systems. While noting the challenges of solid-state experimentation, they emphasize the value of purpose-built open hardware for democratizing science. | Provides a philosophical and practical architecture for building low-cost, modular hardware, offering a blueprint for open-sourcing the AM powder doser. |
| 11 | (c) Self-driving labs / autonomous experimentation | Pelkie et al. 2025, *ChemRxiv*, doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zhkrf | Highlighting user-developed automation, this perspective showcases several open-hardware tools designed to bypass expensive commercial lab equipment (pqac-00000054, pqac-00000056). Notably, it features a ~$300 custom powder dispensing module that uses a stepper-driven precision auger and an integrated balance for closed-loop feedback. The authors stress that rigorous documentation and community sharing are as vital as the hardware design itself. | Offers a direct, proven low-cost gravimetric auger prototype for self-driving labs, directly translating to the design of the AM powder doser. |
| 12 | (c) Self-driving labs / autonomous experimentation | Seifrid et al. 2022, *Accounts of Chemical Research*, 55:2454-2466, doi:10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220 | Discussing the challenges of establishing self-driving labs, the authors identify powder dispensing as a critical motor-function obstacle (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017). Automating the handling of heterogeneous solid systems requires extensive calibration, especially for doses below 20 mg, and cannot simply mimic human protocols. Lack of API support from commercial instrument manufacturers further necessitates custom, hacked solutions. | Pinpoints the control challenges and calibration issues that a custom-built, programmable open-source doser must overcome. |
| 13 | (d) Powder flowability and dosing accuracy | Zegzulka et al. 2020, *Scientific Reports*, 10, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3 | This study evaluates the flowability of ten metal powders for additive manufacturing using multiple characterization methods, including angle of repose and shear cell testers (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). The authors found significant discrepancies in flow classifications depending on the testing equipment and material cohesion. They emphasize that particle shape, size distribution, and specific testing methodologies strictly dictate powder flow behavior in AM feeders. | Demonstrates that irregular and cohesive metal powders require robust, adaptable feeding mechanisms, informing the mechanical requirements of the doser to prevent jamming. |
| 14 | (d) Powder flowability and dosing accuracy | Fathollahi et al. 2020, *AAPS PharmSciTech*, 21, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5 | The authors present a high-precision, volumetric micro-feeder combined with an outlet balance for low-dose powder delivery (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000042). By establishing a displacement-feed factor for various powders, they characterized the density variation and compaction behavior during dispensing. The system successfully fed highly cohesive, low-density powders at rates as low as 1.5 g/h by utilizing closed-loop gravimetric monitoring. | Provides a validated volumetric-to-gravimetric control strategy highly applicable to precisely dosing cohesive AM powders. |
| 15 | (d) Powder flowability and dosing accuracy | Pu et al. 2019, *Advances in Manufacturing*, 7:278-287, doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y | This paper describes an automated, gravimetric powder feeding system built to accelerate the preparation of functionally graded thermoelectric materials (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031). The system coordinates stirrer rotational speed and protruded length to achieve precise, closed-loop dosing with errors under 0.1 mg per component. The automated workflow reduced sample preparation time by 50% while successfully creating distinct compositional gradients. | Shows how combining simple mechanical actuators with precision gravimetric feedback yields extreme accuracy (<0.1 mg), a necessary benchmark for micro-alloying in AM. |


*Table: A summary table of 15 peer-reviewed publications covering multi-material AM, graded alloy discovery, self-driving labs, and powder flowability, detailing their relevance to open-hardware powder doser design.*

function.arguments:
{"answer": "## Literature Review: Automated Powder Dispensing, Dosing, and Feeding for Autonomous Additive Manufacturing Alloy Discovery

The design of a low-cost, open-hardware multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery sits at the intersection of four rapidly evolving research areas: multi-material additive manufacturing, combinatorial alloy discovery, self-driving laboratories, and powder flow science. The following curated set of 15 recent peer-reviewed publications provides the necessary technical foundation across these domains.

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### Detailed Reference Entries

---

**1. Schneck, M., Horn, M., Schmitt, M., Seidel, C., Schlick, G., & Reinhart, G. (2021).** Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. *Progress in Additive Manufacturing*, 6, 881–894. doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2

This review systematically classifies multi-material powder deposition devices for powder bed fusion into three categories: holohedral coaters, nozzle-based dispensers, and masked-deposition concepts (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002). Nozzle-based systems achieve deposition rates of 10–64 mg/s with feature sizes down to 85 µm, while holohedral approaches offer higher volumetric rates (60–290 mm³/s) but less spatial selectivity. The authors rate most multi-material deposition hardware at Manufacturing Readiness Level 4–5, indicating laboratory-stage maturity. Minimum feature sizes and quantitative accuracy metrics remain largely unstudied.

**Relevance:** Establishes the baseline hardware performance envelope (deposition rate, resolution, contamination) against which an open-hardware doser must be benchmarked and identifies specific engineering gaps that low-cost designs can target.

---

**2. Mehrpouya, M., Tuma, D., Vaneker, T., Afrasiabi, M., Bambach, M., & Gibson, I. (2022).** Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques. *Rapid Prototyping Journal*, 28(11), 1–19. doi:10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014

This review covers practical multi-material PBF implementations including dual-hopper mixing systems, Aerosint patterning-drum recoaters for selective voxel deposition, and ultrasonic vibration-assisted nozzle dispensing (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006). The authors highlight that cross-contamination between powders remains a critical unresolved challenge and that most demonstrated systems lack quantitative resolution or positional accuracy specifications. Integration of polymer and metal powders via separate dispensing heads illustrates the complexity of handling dissimilar materials in a single build.

**Relevance:** Provides direct mechanical design inspiration for contamination-free, multi-hopper blending architectures and highlights the need for per-material dispensing heads—a modular design principle essential for the open-hardware doser.

---

**3. Li, Z., Ludwig, A., Savan, A., Springer, H., & Raabe, D. (2018).** Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. *Journal of Materials Research*, 33(19), 3156–3169. doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214

This foundational work compares four combinatorial methods for HEA exploration: rapid alloy prototyping (RAP), diffusion multiples, laser additive manufacturing (LAM), and thin-film co-deposition (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000036). LAM from elemental powder blends is identified as the most versatile bulk route for generating compositionally graded HEA libraries. The authors stress the importance of coupling automated synthesis with CALPHAD-guided theoretical predictions to efficiently navigate the vast HEA compositional space.

**Relevance:** Provides the metallurgical rationale for building multi-powder feeding systems—LAM with elemental powders is identified as the preferred route for bulk HEA discovery, directly motivating the targeted doser design.

---

**4. Pegues, J.W., Melia, M.A., Puckett, R., Whetten, S.R., Argibay, N., & Kustas, A.B. (2021).** Exploring additive manufacturing as a high-throughput screening tool for multiphase high entropy alloys. *Additive Manufacturing*, 37, 101598. doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598

The authors demonstrate laser-based DED for rapid HEA screening by in-situ alloying with independently controlled powder flow rates from multiple hoppers (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000038). They fabricated compositionally graded CoCrFeMnNi-based specimens with refractory additions (Ta, Nb, Ti-6Al-4V) and mapped phase stability and hardness as functions of composition. The methodology enabled rapid identification of multiphase regimes across the HEA design space using a single compact specimen.

**Relevance:** Validates that independent flow-rate control across multiple powder hoppers is the core capability required for combinatorial HEA screening—exactly the function the open-hardware doser must deliver.

---

**5. Vecchio, K.S., Dippo, O.F., Kaufmann, K.R., & Liu, X. (2021).** High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ). *Acta Materialia*, 221, 117352. doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352

HT-READ integrates a 16-vial automated Alloy Development Feeder (ADF) with DED fabrication, CALPHAD screening, and machine-learning analysis into a closed-loop workflow (pqac-00000047, pqac-00000049). The ADF rotates under computer control to feed one composition at a time, producing ring-shaped 16-sample libraries that enable fully automated XRD and SEM-EDS characterization without sample realignment. ML-driven hardness prediction using Automatminer correctly identified three of the five hardest alloy compositions.

**Relevance:** Showcases the most complete automated alloy-discovery pipeline to date; replicating the 16-vial automated feeder concept via low-cost open hardware would democratize this state-of-the-art workflow.

---

**6. Nelaturu, P., Hattrick-Simpers, J.R., Moorehead, M., Jambur, V., Szlufarska, I., Couet, A., & Thoma, D.J. (2024).** Multi-principal element alloy discovery using directed energy deposition and machine learning. *Materials Science and Engineering: A*, 891, 145945. doi:10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945

This study demonstrates an active-learning-driven DED workflow using an Optomec LENS system with four independent auger-driven powder hoppers containing elemental Cr, Fe, Mn, and Ni powders (pqac-00000009). Composition control was achieved by varying auger RPM, with mass flow scaling linearly and compositional accuracy within ±5 at%. The closed-loop ML approach synthesized and characterized over 100 alloy compositions within a single week.

**Relevance:** Proves that simple auger-driven feeders deliver sufficient precision (±5 at%) for ML-guided alloy discovery, validating the auger as a practical, low-cost metering mechanism for the open-hardware doser.

---

**7. Li, J., Zhang, X., An, Z., Li, B., Wang, Y., Yang, Y., Tong, K., & Zhu, Y. (2025).** High-throughput phase screening and laser-directed energy deposition of Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys. *Coatings*, 15(4), 401. doi:10.3390/coatings15040401

The authors developed an integrated DED system featuring a multi-powder feeder with real-time continuously variable composition control, an in-line multi-powder mixer, and a custom coaxial nozzle (pqac-00000011). They fabricated Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys in a single deposition track with verified phase transitions from Nb-rich intermetallics to Ti-rich phases. CFD simulations were used to optimize powder mixer geometry for flow homogeneity.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that precision dosing alone is insufficient—an in-line powder mixing module is essential for compositional homogeneity, informing the system architecture of the open-hardware doser.

---

**8. Liu, M., Lei, C., Wang, Y., Zhang, B., & Qu, X. (2024).** High-throughput preparation for alloy composition design in additive manufacturing: a comprehensive review. *Materials Genome Engineering Advances*, 2(3). doi:10.1002/mgea.55

This review evaluates high-throughput DED and LPBF methods for alloy development across aluminum, titanium, nickel superalloys, and HEAs (pqac-00000008). It contrasts multi-hopper layer-by-layer approaches (Z-axis gradients) with gradient powder spreading (horizontal gradients) and identifies real-time composition control as a primary bottleneck. The review emphasizes the potential of AM-based high-throughput workflows to replace inefficient trial-and-error alloy development.

**Relevance:** Identifies real-time composition control as the key capability gap in current AM alloy development, directly motivating the open-hardware doser's core design objective.

---

**9. Tom, G., Schmid, S.P., Baird, S.G., et al. (2024).** Self-driving laboratories for chemistry and materials science. *Chemical Reviews*, 124(16), 9633–9732. doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.4c00055

This comprehensive review of SDL technology identifies solid powder dispensing as substantially more challenging than liquid handling, requiring real-time mass measurement and material-specific calibration (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000057). The authors document how high commercial automation costs are driving development of open-source, 3D-printed hardware solutions. Examples include dual-arm robotic manipulators for solid dispensing and mechanical approaches such as scraping and grinding.

**Relevance:** Confirms that automated powder handling is a recognized major barrier in SDLs, strongly supporting the development of purpose-built open-source hardware solutions for powder dosing.

---

**10. Lo, S., Baird, S.G., Schrier, J., et al. (2024).** Review of low-cost self-driving laboratories in chemistry and materials science: the "frugal twin" concept. *Digital Discovery*, 3(5), 842–868. doi:10.1039/d3dd00223c

This paper proposes the "frugal twin" concept—low-cost physical surrogates of expensive experimental platforms—and reviews open-hardware SDL projects built from RepRap printers, Arduino microcontrollers, and 3D-printed parts (pqac-00000058). The Jubilee tool-changing platform is highlighted as a ~$2000 modular workcell. The authors note that extending SDL capabilities to solid-state materials (e.g., colored wax powder mixing) remains an open challenge requiring purpose-built powder-handling modules.

**Relevance:** Provides the architectural philosophy (modular, open-source, frugal) and identifies specific platforms (Jubilee) onto which a multi-powder doser module could be integrated.

---

**11. Pelkie, B., Baird, S., Aissi, E., et al. (2025).** Democratizing self-driving labs through user-developed automation infrastructure. *ChemRxiv*. doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zhkrf

This workshop perspective showcases 14 user-developed SDL tools, including a custom powder dispensing module (~$300, ~10 hours to reproduce) that uses a stepper-motor-driven precision auger with an integrated balance for closed-loop gravimetric feedback (pqac-00000054, pqac-00000056). The dispensed powder is collected in disposable syringe bodies and mixed with liquid via peristaltic pump. The authors emphasize that rigorous documentation and community sharing are as critical as the hardware design itself.

**Relevance:** Offers the closest existing open-hardware precedent to the envisioned multi-powder doser—a proven, inexpensive gravimetric auger system that can be directly adapted and extended for multi-channel AM powder feeding.

---

**12. Seifrid, M., Pollice, R., Aguilar-Granda, A., et al. (2022).** Autonomous chemical experiments: challenges and perspectives on establishing a self-driving lab. *Accounts of Chemical Research*, 55(17), 2454–2466. doi:10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220

The authors identify powder dispensing as a critical "motor function" challenge for SDLs, noting that reliably dispensing masses below ~20 mg and handling hundreds of different solids with varying physical properties demands impractical levels of calibration (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017). They argue that experimental protocols must be fundamentally redesigned for automation rather than simply copied from manual workflows. Many commercial instruments lack APIs or manufacturer support for external control, necessitating custom integration.

**Relevance:** Pinpoints the specific precision and calibration challenges that the open-hardware doser must address, particularly for sub-20 mg doses of diverse metal powders.

---

**13. Zegzulka, J., Gelnar, D., Jezerska, L., Prokes, R., & Rozbroj, J. (2020).** Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. *Scientific Reports*, 10. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3

This study characterizes ten metal powders (316L, Zn, Sn, Al, Cu, Mn, Fe, Bronze, Ti, Mo) using multiple flowability methods including angle of repose, shear cell testing with three different instruments (RST-01.pc, Brookfield PFT, FT4), and particle size/shape analysis (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000025). Results showed significant device-to-device discrepancies: flow index classifications varied from 100% free-flowing (RST) to only 50% free-flowing (FT4) for the same powder set. Particle sphericity and size distribution were strongly correlated with flowability.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that metal AM powders span a wide flowability range and that irregular, non-spherical powders (Ti, Mo, Bronze) require robust, adaptable feeding mechanisms—critical design input for preventing jamming in the multi-powder doser.

---

**14. Fathollahi, S., Sacher, S., Escotet-Espinoza, M.S., DiNunzio, J., & Khinast, J.G. (2020).** Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. *AAPS PharmSciTech*, 21(8). doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5

This paper presents a volumetric micro-feeder combining piston displacement with an outlet weighing balance, achieving continuous low-dose feeding from ~1.5 to 15 g/h (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000042). The authors introduce a "displacement-feed factor" profile for each powder that captures in-process densification and can be used for calibration at any target feed rate. The system successfully handled highly cohesive, low-density powders (SiO₂ at 0.04 g/cm³) without significant fluctuations, and requires only tens of milliliters of material for characterization.

**Relevance:** Provides a validated volumetric-to-gravimetric control strategy directly applicable to dosing cohesive AM powders, and the displacement-feed factor concept offers a calibration methodology transferable to the open-hardware doser.

---

**15. Pu, H.-Y., Xie, R.-Q., Peng, Y., Yang, Y., He, S.-Y., Luo, J., Sun, Y., Xie, S.-R., & Luo, J. (2019).** Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. *Advances in Manufacturing*, 7(3), 278–287. doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y

The authors built a three-channel automated gravimetric powder feeding system using PLC control, precision balances (0.01 mg readability), and a two-stage feeding strategy (fast coarse + precise PID-controlled fine) (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000031). Feeding rates ranged from 0.19 to 15.27 mg/s, with per-component dosing errors below 0.1 mg. The system was validated by producing graded Bi_xSb_{2-x}Te_3 samples with confirmed compositional gradients, reducing preparation time by ~50% versus manual weighing.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that combining simple mechanical actuators (stirrer + protruded groove) with precision gravimetric feedback achieves extreme accuracy (<0.1 mg)—the gold standard for micro-alloying in AM powder dosing and a directly replicable architecture for the open-hardware doser.

---

### Synthesis and Design Implications

Collectively, these references reveal several convergent design principles for a low-cost open-hardware multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery:

1. **Metering mechanism:** Auger-driven and stirrer-based volumetric feeders have been validated for compositional accuracy within ±5 at% (pqac-00000009) and dosing errors below 0.1 mg (pqac-00000028), making them the preferred low-cost metering approach.

2. **Closed-loop gravimetric control:** Nearly all successful systems employ a precision balance for real-time feedback, converting volumetric displacement to accurate mass delivery (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000039).

3. **Modularity and interchangeable heads:** Powder-specific dispensing heads are essential because flowability varies dramatically across AM-relevant metal powders (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000054).

4. **In-line mixing:** Homogeneous alloy deposition requires not only accurate dosing but also effective in-line powder mixing downstream of the feeders (pqac-00000011).

5. **Open-source ecosystem:** The SDL community has demonstrated that $300–$2000 open-hardware platforms can achieve research-grade performance when paired with rigorous documentation and community support (pqac-00000054, pqac-00000058).

6. **Autonomy-ready integration:** Software APIs, MQTT communication, and compatibility with tool-changing motion platforms (e.g., Jubilee) are critical for integrating the doser into closed-loop autonomous experimentation workflows (pqac-00000052)."}

{"answer": "## Literature Review: Automated Powder Dispensing, Dosing, and Feeding for Autonomous Additive Manufacturing Alloy Discovery

The design of a low-cost, open-hardware multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery sits at the intersection of four rapidly evolving research areas: multi-material additive manufacturing, combinatorial alloy discovery, self-driving laboratories, and powder flow science. The following curated set of 15 recent peer-reviewed publications provides the necessary technical foundation across these domains.

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### Detailed Reference Entries

---

**1. Schneck, M., Horn, M., Schmitt, M., Seidel, C., Schlick, G., & Reinhart, G. (2021).** Review on additive hybrid- and multi-material-manufacturing of metals by powder bed fusion: state of technology and development potential. *Progress in Additive Manufacturing*, 6, 881–894. doi:10.1007/s40964-021-00205-2

This review systematically classifies multi-material powder deposition devices for powder bed fusion into three categories: holohedral coaters, nozzle-based dispensers, and masked-deposition concepts (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002). Nozzle-based systems achieve deposition rates of 10–64 mg/s with feature sizes down to 85 µm, while holohedral approaches offer higher volumetric rates (60–290 mm³/s) but less spatial selectivity. The authors rate most multi-material deposition hardware at Manufacturing Readiness Level 4–5, indicating laboratory-stage maturity. Minimum feature sizes and quantitative accuracy metrics remain largely unstudied.

**Relevance:** Establishes the baseline hardware performance envelope (deposition rate, resolution, contamination) against which an open-hardware doser must be benchmarked and identifies specific engineering gaps that low-cost designs can target.

---

**2. Mehrpouya, M., Tuma, D., Vaneker, T., Afrasiabi, M., Bambach, M., & Gibson, I. (2022).** Multimaterial powder bed fusion techniques. *Rapid Prototyping Journal*, 28(11), 1–19. doi:10.1108/rpj-01-2022-0014

This review covers practical multi-material PBF implementations including dual-hopper mixing systems, Aerosint patterning-drum recoaters for selective voxel deposition, and ultrasonic vibration-assisted nozzle dispensing (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006). The authors highlight that cross-contamination between powders remains a critical unresolved challenge and that most demonstrated systems lack quantitative resolution or positional accuracy specifications. Integration of polymer and metal powders via separate dispensing heads illustrates the complexity of handling dissimilar materials in a single build.

**Relevance:** Provides direct mechanical design inspiration for contamination-free, multi-hopper blending architectures and highlights the need for per-material dispensing heads—a modular design principle essential for the open-hardware doser.

---

**3. Li, Z., Ludwig, A., Savan, A., Springer, H., & Raabe, D. (2018).** Combinatorial metallurgical synthesis and processing of high-entropy alloys. *Journal of Materials Research*, 33(19), 3156–3169. doi:10.1557/jmr.2018.214

This foundational work compares four combinatorial methods for HEA exploration: rapid alloy prototyping (RAP), diffusion multiples, laser additive manufacturing (LAM), and thin-film co-deposition (pqac-00000034, pqac-00000036). LAM from elemental powder blends is identified as the most versatile bulk route for generating compositionally graded HEA libraries. The authors stress the importance of coupling automated synthesis with CALPHAD-guided theoretical predictions to efficiently navigate the vast HEA compositional space.

**Relevance:** Provides the metallurgical rationale for building multi-powder feeding systems—LAM with elemental powders is identified as the preferred route for bulk HEA discovery, directly motivating the targeted doser design.

---

**4. Pegues, J.W., Melia, M.A., Puckett, R., Whetten, S.R., Argibay, N., & Kustas, A.B. (2021).** Exploring additive manufacturing as a high-throughput screening tool for multiphase high entropy alloys. *Additive Manufacturing*, 37, 101598. doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101598

The authors demonstrate laser-based DED for rapid HEA screening by in-situ alloying with independently controlled powder flow rates from multiple hoppers (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000038). They fabricated compositionally graded CoCrFeMnNi-based specimens with refractory additions (Ta, Nb, Ti-6Al-4V) and mapped phase stability and hardness as functions of composition. The methodology enabled rapid identification of multiphase regimes across the HEA design space using a single compact specimen.

**Relevance:** Validates that independent flow-rate control across multiple powder hoppers is the core capability required for combinatorial HEA screening—exactly the function the open-hardware doser must deliver.

---

**5. Vecchio, K.S., Dippo, O.F., Kaufmann, K.R., & Liu, X. (2021).** High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ). *Acta Materialia*, 221, 117352. doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352

HT-READ integrates a 16-vial automated Alloy Development Feeder (ADF) with DED fabrication, CALPHAD screening, and machine-learning analysis into a closed-loop workflow (pqac-00000047, pqac-00000049). The ADF rotates under computer control to feed one composition at a time, producing ring-shaped 16-sample libraries that enable fully automated XRD and SEM-EDS characterization without sample realignment. ML-driven hardness prediction using Automatminer correctly identified three of the five hardest alloy compositions.

**Relevance:** Showcases the most complete automated alloy-discovery pipeline to date; replicating the 16-vial automated feeder concept via low-cost open hardware would democratize this state-of-the-art workflow.

---

**6. Nelaturu, P., Hattrick-Simpers, J.R., Moorehead, M., Jambur, V., Szlufarska, I., Couet, A., & Thoma, D.J. (2024).** Multi-principal element alloy discovery using directed energy deposition and machine learning. *Materials Science and Engineering: A*, 891, 145945. doi:10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945

This study demonstrates an active-learning-driven DED workflow using an Optomec LENS system with four independent auger-driven powder hoppers containing elemental Cr, Fe, Mn, and Ni powders (pqac-00000009). Composition control was achieved by varying auger RPM, with mass flow scaling linearly and compositional accuracy within ±5 at%. The closed-loop ML approach synthesized and characterized over 100 alloy compositions within a single week.

**Relevance:** Proves that simple auger-driven feeders deliver sufficient precision (±5 at%) for ML-guided alloy discovery, validating the auger as a practical, low-cost metering mechanism for the open-hardware doser.

---

**7. Li, J., Zhang, X., An, Z., Li, B., Wang, Y., Yang, Y., Tong, K., & Zhu, Y. (2025).** High-throughput phase screening and laser-directed energy deposition of Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys. *Coatings*, 15(4), 401. doi:10.3390/coatings15040401

The authors developed an integrated DED system featuring a multi-powder feeder with real-time continuously variable composition control, an in-line multi-powder mixer, and a custom coaxial nozzle (pqac-00000011). They fabricated Ti-Ni-Nb gradient alloys in a single deposition track with verified phase transitions from Nb-rich intermetallics to Ti-rich phases. CFD simulations were used to optimize powder mixer geometry for flow homogeneity.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that precision dosing alone is insufficient—an in-line powder mixing module is essential for compositional homogeneity, informing the system architecture of the open-hardware doser.

---

**8. Liu, M., Lei, C., Wang, Y., Zhang, B., & Qu, X. (2024).** High-throughput preparation for alloy composition design in additive manufacturing: a comprehensive review. *Materials Genome Engineering Advances*, 2(3). doi:10.1002/mgea.55

This review evaluates high-throughput DED and LPBF methods for alloy development across aluminum, titanium, nickel superalloys, and HEAs (pqac-00000008). It contrasts multi-hopper layer-by-layer approaches (Z-axis gradients) with gradient powder spreading (horizontal gradients) and identifies real-time composition control as a primary bottleneck. The review emphasizes the potential of AM-based high-throughput workflows to replace inefficient trial-and-error alloy development.

**Relevance:** Identifies real-time composition control as the key capability gap in current AM alloy development, directly motivating the open-hardware doser's core design objective.

---

**9. Tom, G., Schmid, S.P., Baird, S.G., et al. (2024).** Self-driving laboratories for chemistry and materials science. *Chemical Reviews*, 124(16), 9633–9732. doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.4c00055

This comprehensive review of SDL technology identifies solid powder dispensing as substantially more challenging than liquid handling, requiring real-time mass measurement and material-specific calibration (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000057). The authors document how high commercial automation costs are driving development of open-source, 3D-printed hardware solutions. Examples include dual-arm robotic manipulators for solid dispensing and mechanical approaches such as scraping and grinding.

**Relevance:** Confirms that automated powder handling is a recognized major barrier in SDLs, strongly supporting the development of purpose-built open-source hardware solutions for powder dosing.

---

**10. Lo, S., Baird, S.G., Schrier, J., et al. (2024).** Review of low-cost self-driving laboratories in chemistry and materials science: the "frugal twin" concept. *Digital Discovery*, 3(5), 842–868. doi:10.1039/d3dd00223c

This paper proposes the "frugal twin" concept—low-cost physical surrogates of expensive experimental platforms—and reviews open-hardware SDL projects built from RepRap printers, Arduino microcontrollers, and 3D-printed parts (pqac-00000058). The Jubilee tool-changing platform is highlighted as a ~$2000 modular workcell. The authors note that extending SDL capabilities to solid-state materials (e.g., colored wax powder mixing) remains an open challenge requiring purpose-built powder-handling modules.

**Relevance:** Provides the architectural philosophy (modular, open-source, frugal) and identifies specific platforms (Jubilee) onto which a multi-powder doser module could be integrated.

---

**11. Pelkie, B., Baird, S., Aissi, E., et al. (2025).** Democratizing self-driving labs through user-developed automation infrastructure. *ChemRxiv*. doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zhkrf

This workshop perspective showcases 14 user-developed SDL tools, including a custom powder dispensing module (~$300, ~10 hours to reproduce) that uses a stepper-motor-driven precision auger with an integrated balance for closed-loop gravimetric feedback (pqac-00000054, pqac-00000056). The dispensed powder is collected in disposable syringe bodies and mixed with liquid via peristaltic pump. The authors emphasize that rigorous documentation and community sharing are as critical as the hardware design itself.

**Relevance:** Offers the closest existing open-hardware precedent to the envisioned multi-powder doser—a proven, inexpensive gravimetric auger system that can be directly adapted and extended for multi-channel AM powder feeding.

---

**12. Seifrid, M., Pollice, R., Aguilar-Granda, A., et al. (2022).** Autonomous chemical experiments: challenges and perspectives on establishing a self-driving lab. *Accounts of Chemical Research*, 55(17), 2454–2466. doi:10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00220

The authors identify powder dispensing as a critical "motor function" challenge for SDLs, noting that reliably dispensing masses below ~20 mg and handling hundreds of different solids with varying physical properties demands impractical levels of calibration (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017). They argue that experimental protocols must be fundamentally redesigned for automation rather than simply copied from manual workflows. Many commercial instruments lack APIs or manufacturer support for external control, necessitating custom integration.

**Relevance:** Pinpoints the specific precision and calibration challenges that the open-hardware doser must address, particularly for sub-20 mg doses of diverse metal powders.

---

**13. Zegzulka, J., Gelnar, D., Jezerska, L., Prokes, R., & Rozbroj, J. (2020).** Characterization and flowability methods for metal powders. *Scientific Reports*, 10. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-77974-3

This study characterizes ten metal powders (316L, Zn, Sn, Al, Cu, Mn, Fe, Bronze, Ti, Mo) using multiple flowability methods including angle of repose, shear cell testing with three different instruments (RST-01.pc, Brookfield PFT, FT4), and particle size/shape analysis (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000025). Results showed significant device-to-device discrepancies: flow index classifications varied from 100% free-flowing (RST) to only 50% free-flowing (FT4) for the same powder set. Particle sphericity and size distribution were strongly correlated with flowability.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that metal AM powders span a wide flowability range and that irregular, non-spherical powders (Ti, Mo, Bronze) require robust, adaptable feeding mechanisms—critical design input for preventing jamming in the multi-powder doser.

---

**14. Fathollahi, S., Sacher, S., Escotet-Espinoza, M.S., DiNunzio, J., & Khinast, J.G. (2020).** Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. *AAPS PharmSciTech*, 21(8). doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5

This paper presents a volumetric micro-feeder combining piston displacement with an outlet weighing balance, achieving continuous low-dose feeding from ~1.5 to 15 g/h (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000042). The authors introduce a "displacement-feed factor" profile for each powder that captures in-process densification and can be used for calibration at any target feed rate. The system successfully handled highly cohesive, low-density powders (SiO₂ at 0.04 g/cm³) without significant fluctuations, and requires only tens of milliliters of material for characterization.

**Relevance:** Provides a validated volumetric-to-gravimetric control strategy directly applicable to dosing cohesive AM powders, and the displacement-feed factor concept offers a calibration methodology transferable to the open-hardware doser.

---

**15. Pu, H.-Y., Xie, R.-Q., Peng, Y., Yang, Y., He, S.-Y., Luo, J., Sun, Y., Xie, S.-R., & Luo, J. (2019).** Accelerating sample preparation of graded thermoelectric materials using an automatic powder feeding system. *Advances in Manufacturing*, 7(3), 278–287. doi:10.1007/s40436-019-00269-y

The authors built a three-channel automated gravimetric powder feeding system using PLC control, precision balances (0.01 mg readability), and a two-stage feeding strategy (fast coarse + precise PID-controlled fine) (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000031). Feeding rates ranged from 0.19 to 15.27 mg/s, with per-component dosing errors below 0.1 mg. The system was validated by producing graded Bi_xSb_{2-x}Te_3 samples with confirmed compositional gradients, reducing preparation time by ~50% versus manual weighing.

**Relevance:** Demonstrates that combining simple mechanical actuators (stirrer + protruded groove) with precision gravimetric feedback achieves extreme accuracy (<0.1 mg)—the gold standard for micro-alloying in AM powder dosing and a directly replicable architecture for the open-hardware doser.

---

### Synthesis and Design Implications

Collectively, these references reveal several convergent design principles for a low-cost open-hardware multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery:

1. **Metering mechanism:** Auger-driven and stirrer-based volumetric feeders have been validated for compositional accuracy within ±5 at% (pqac-00000009) and dosing errors below 0.1 mg (pqac-00000028), making them the preferred low-cost metering approach.

2. **Closed-loop gravimetric control:** Nearly all successful systems employ a precision balance for real-time feedback, converting volumetric displacement to accurate mass delivery (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000039).

3. **Modularity and interchangeable heads:** Powder-specific dispensing heads are essential because flowability varies dramatically across AM-relevant metal powders (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000054).

4. **In-line mixing:** Homogeneous alloy deposition requires not only accurate dosing but also effective in-line powder mixing downstream of the feeders (pqac-00000011).

5. **Open-source ecosystem:** The SDL community has demonstrated that $300–$2000 open-hardware platforms can achieve research-grade performance when paired with rigorous documentation and community support (pqac-00000054, pqac-00000058).

6. **Autonomy-ready integration:** Software APIs, MQTT communication, and compatibility with tool-changing motion platforms (e.g., Jubilee) are critical for integrating the doser into closed-loop autonomous experimentation workflows (pqac-00000052)."}
